text:
One lesson I’ve learned:

When everyone crowds into the same sectors, it’s worth looking elsewhere.

Today that’s AI, semiconductors, photonics, and software.

Tomorrow it could be retail, beauty, home furnishings, travel, or consumer brands.

Great investments don’t always come from the most complex businesses.

Sometimes they come from companies with products people buy every day.

Keep an open mind.

#Investing #Stocks #Diversification $ELF

text:
🚨 SCIENTISTS JUST TURNED A SINGLE PHOTO INTO A FULL 3D SCENE.

Researchers have developed a diffractive decoder a specially engineered optical element that can reconstruct complete 3D scenes from just one 2D image.

Instead of relying on multiple cameras, structured light, or slow scanning systems, this approach uses a passive diffractive layer combined with computational decoding to recover depth information instantly.

Why this matters:
• Traditional 3D imaging often requires bulky equipment, multiple viewpoints, or time-consuming scanning
• This system works in a single snapshot, making it extremely fast
• The diffractive decoder is thin, lightweight, and potentially low-cost to manufacture
• It opens the door to real-time 3D imaging in applications where size, speed, and simplicity matter

The deeper implication:
We are moving toward a future where capturing accurate 3D information no longer requires complex hardware setups.

A single thin optical layer + smart computation could enable fast 3D vision in drones, medical devices, autonomous vehicles, robotics, and augmented reality systems.

This is the kind of foundational optical breakthrough that often becomes invisible infrastructure quietly enabling many future technologies.
What application do you think would benefit most from fast, single-shot 3D imaging?

Follow for more frontier optics, imaging, and photonics research.

Researchers have developed a diffractive decoder a specially engineered optical element that can reconstruct complete 3D scenes from just one 2D image. Instead of relying on multiple cameras, structured light, or slow scanning systems, this approach uses a passive diffractive layer combined with computational decoding to recover depth information instantly. Why this matters: • Traditional 3D imaging often requires bulky equipment, multiple viewpoints, or time-consuming scanning • This system works in a single snapshot, making it extremely fast • The diffractive decoder is thin, lightweight, and potentially low-cost to manufacture • It opens the door to real-time 3D imaging in applications where size, speed, and simplicity matter The deeper implication: We are moving toward a future where capturing accurate 3D information no longer requires complex hardware setups. A single thin optical layer + smart computation could enable fast 3D vision in drones, medical devices, autonomous vehicles, robotics, and augmented reality systems. This is the kind of foundational optical breakthrough that often becomes invisible infrastructure quietly enabling many future technologies. What application do you think would benefit most from fast, single-shot 3D imaging? text:
There's a flaw in the market here.

1 of these 2 groups has to go up, but both are down.

That means all of these stocks are being undervalued right now.

But that's impossible in the future.

So, I'm loading up on these stocks here (with theses):

AI beneficiaries:

$ZETA - digital marketing platform whose earnings have grown tremendously over the past few years.

$NOW - enterprise workflow software becoming the operating system for AI-powered businesses.

$TEAM - collaboration software platform embedding AI across workplace productivity and development tools.

$SOFI - digital financial platform using AI to scale lending, banking, and member growth.

$PLTR - AI operating system helping governments and enterprises make decisions from massive datasets.

AI enablers:

$BRUN - advanced cooling solutions provider helping solve AI datacenter power and heat challenges.

$OPTX - photonics company enabling faster and more efficient AI data transmission.

$ASYS - semiconductor equipment supplier benefiting from growing AI chip production demand.

$SHMD - specialty materials company supplying critical components for advanced semiconductor manufacturing.

$AAOI - optical networking company providing high-speed transceivers powering AI infrastructure.

Other very notable names in these niches:

$SIVE - silicon photonics company developing next-generation AI networking technology.

$DGXX - digital infrastructure company building critical connectivity for AI and cloud workloads.

$HIVE - AI-focused datacenter operator monetizing growing compute demand.

$NBIS - AI cloud infrastructure provider delivering compute capacity for model training and inference.

$IREN - renewable-powered AI datacenter operator expanding high-performance computing capacity.

$CRWV - hyperscale AI cloud platform supplying infrastructure to leading AI developers.

$MARA - digital infrastructure company leveraging power and compute assets for AI opportunities.

$CLSK - energy-efficient infrastructure operator positioned to benefit from rising compute demand.

$AOSL - power semiconductor company supplying components essential for AI hardware.

$IQE - compound semiconductor materials provider supporting photonics and advanced chip technologies.

$XFAB - specialty semiconductor foundry manufacturing chips used across AI and industrial markets.

$DUOL - education platform using AI to improve personalization, engagement, and learning outcomes.

$RDDT - social platform monetizing unique data and advertising opportunities in the AI era.

$INTU - financial software leader embedding AI throughout consumer and enterprise products.

$ADBE - creative software giant integrating generative AI across its product ecosystem.

$CRM - customer relationship software leader using AI to automate sales and service workflows.

$HUBS - marketing software platform leveraging AI to improve customer acquisition and engagement.

$WDAY - enterprise HR and finance platform deploying AI to enhance workforce productivity.

$APP - advertising technology company using AI to optimize targeting and monetization.

$BRZE - customer engagement platform applying AI to improve marketing performance and retention.

$TYL - government software provider embedding AI into public sector workflows and operations.

I'm still so surprised both of these niches are at discounted prices.

Especially as both of them should grow tremendously in the future (read thesis below).

What are your thoughts?

text:
🚨 NEW: GlobalFoundries Launches Dedicated Quantum Manufacturing Arm

GlobalFoundries has officially launched Quantum Technology Solutions, a new business unit designed to act as a dedicated manufacturing foundry for the entire quantum computing ecosystem. The initiative is backed by a massive $375 million CHIPS R&D grant from the U.S. Department of Commerce.

The U.S. government is taking an equity investment in the company as part of a strategic, long-term push to keep critical quantum manufacturing and infrastructure on American soil.

The new unit will mass-produce quantum processors, control chips, and interconnects across multiple qubit modalities using standard 300mm production lines.

The division is launching with customers working on nearly every competing quantum approach, including photonics, trapped ions, and silicon spin qubits.

Public supporters and early customers of the new foundry include tech giants like Google Quantum AI, Microsoft, and Nvidia, alongside specialized startups like PsiQuantum and Quantinuum.

"Qubit counts and error-rate requirements are reaching the threshold where manufacturing now needs to lead the approach. A million-qubit system can’t be built in a lab." — Nicholas Sergeant

By centralizing the fabrication of quantum components, GlobalFoundries is separating design from manufacturing—the exact same structural shift that turned classical semiconductors into a trillion-dollar global ecosystem. Establishing a reliable, domestic supply chain will be the defining factor in accelerating quantum computing from bespoke, hand-assembled lab prototypes to utility-scale commercial reality.

Source: Forbes Innovation

text:
Today, the Nasdaq Composite looked like a V: down more than 4% by lunchtime, closing off just 1%. 

Nobody knows exactly why.

Around noon, the AI jitters came back, and traders dumped the highest-beta names—the frothiest, most volatile stuff, like Strategy (MSTR), the leveraged bitcoin vehicle that had popped Monday; AppLovin (APP); the photonics maker Lumentum (LITE). 

But the densest cluster was the chipmakers: Marvell, which dropped 10% a day after jumping 10% on news it’s joining the S&P 500, and the rest of what strategist Ben Emons has dubbed the “Parabolic 7,” after a chip index that ran up nearly 100% in a matter of weeks. 

Rather than fleeing equities altogether—treasuries barely moved—the market rotated into peanut butter and paint. Smucker jumped double digits; Home Depot and Sherwin-Williams led. Real estate, staples, and utilities finished up: the classic ballast against tech froth.

“You’re seeing money flow into consumer names that have been unwanted and unloved,” Richard Steinberg, senior global market strategist at Focus Partners Wealth, told the WSJ.

The trigger wasn’t clear, but there are real reasons to want out of chips and diversified away from the AI trade. Wall Street has to make room for SpaceX, set to be the largest IPO ever on Friday, with OpenAI and Anthropic—both now confidentially filed—close behind. Annex Wealth’s Brian Jacobsen called the tech run an Icarus trade with the wings melting: Alphabet’s rare capital raise was the first warning, SpaceX the “shiny new toys” pulling money out. Though even that story has a wrinkle: SpaceX is already oversubscribed, with multiple $10 billion orders in.

Also this week, inflation data lands Wednesday and Thursday, and a strong May jobs report last week has already pushed expectations for rate cuts further out. Funds tend not to sit in their most crowded, highest-beta positions going into an inflation reading that could move the Fed’s path, which could be why some froth got cut off now. 

The selling may also be less about selling than an absence of buyers. Founder ETFs’ Michael Monaghan described it as buyers stepping back rather than a rush for the exits, dropping the price faster than the volume would suggest. 

Oil went the other way. Even after President Donald Trump said the U.S. would have to respond to Iran’s downing of a U.S. Army Apache helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz, crude fell about 3% to roughly $88, as the energy secretary said traffic through the Strait was picking up meaningfully. 

Certainly investors are now wondering if this is the start of a longer “pop” or just a one-time correction. For them, the best information will come as SpaceX debuts, and public capital gets to decide if they believe in the AI story or not.

Today, the Nasdaq Composite looked like a V: down more than 4% by lunchtime, closing off just 1%. 

Nobody knows exactly why.

Around noon, the AI jitters came back, and traders dumped the highest-beta names—the frothiest, most volatile stuff, like Strategy (MSTR), the leveraged bitcoin vehicle that had popped Monday; AppLovin (APP); the photonics maker Lumentum (LITE). 

But the densest cluster was the chipmakers: Marvell, which dropped 10% a day after jumping 10% on news it’s joining the S&P 500, and the rest of what strategist Ben Emons has dubbed the “Parabolic 7,” after a chip index that ran up nearly 100% in a matter of weeks. 

Rather than fleeing equities altogether—treasuries barely moved—the market rotated into peanut butter and paint. Smucker jumped double digits; Home Depot and Sherwin-Williams led. Real estate, staples, and utilities finished up: the classic ballast against tech froth.

“You’re seeing money flow into consumer names that have been unwanted and unloved,” Richard Steinberg, senior global market strategist at Focus Partners Wealth, told the WSJ.

The trigger wasn’t clear, but there are real reasons to want out of chips and diversified away from the AI trade. Wall Street has to make room for SpaceX, set to be the largest IPO ever on Friday, with OpenAI and Anthropic—both now confidentially filed—close behind. Annex Wealth’s Brian Jacobsen called the tech run an Icarus trade with the wings melting: Alphabet’s rare capital raise was the first warning, SpaceX the “shiny new toys” pulling money out. Though even that story has a wrinkle: SpaceX is already oversubscribed, with multiple $10 billion orders in.

Also this week, inflation data lands Wednesday and Thursday, and a strong May jobs report last week has already pushed expectations for rate cuts further out. Funds tend not to sit in their most crowded, highest-beta positions going into an inflation reading that could move the Fed’s path, which could be why some froth got cut off now. 

The selling may also be less about selling than an absence of buyers. Founder ETFs’ Michael Monaghan described it as buyers stepping back rather than a rush for the exits, dropping the price faster than the volume would suggest. 

Oil went the other way. Even after President Donald Trump said the U.S. would have to respond to Iran’s downing of a U.S. Army Apache helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz, crude fell about 3% to roughly $88, as the energy secretary said traffic through the Strait was picking up meaningfully. 

Certainly investors are now wondering if this is the start of a longer “pop” or just a one-time correction. For them, the best information will come as SpaceX debuts, and public capital gets to decide if they believe in the AI story or not.

This story was originally featured on https://t.co/opgY7EYkBs

~95% revenue concentration in top 3 customers. Insiders sold ~$12.6M in May after the run. EPS is still barely breakeven near-term. Analyst targets range from $129 (B. Riley, Neutral) to $220+ — wide dispersion.

This is not a bet-the-farm swing. Size it like one.

text:
$IONQ The military use case for quantum technology is becoming one of the most important investment themes in the sector.

Most investors focus on quantum computing. Governments and defense agencies are increasingly focused on quantum sensing, quantum networking, secure communications, and GPS-independent navigation.

According to recent analysis from the Center for Strategic and International Studies, quantum sensing has the potential to reshape modern warfare by enabling detection capabilities that conventional systems struggle to achieve. Applications include:

• Detecting submarines through quantum magnetometers
• Identifying underground tunnels and hidden infrastructure through quantum gravimeters
• GPS-denied navigation using atomic clocks and inertial sensors
• Enhanced intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR)
• Quantum-secure communications and networking
The strategic implication is simple:
Many of today's military advantages depend on remaining undetected.

If quantum sensing matures as expected, stealth aircraft, submarines, underground facilities, and even electronic warfare tactics could become more vulnerable to detection. CSIS compares the potential impact to the way radar transformed warfare in the 20th century.

This is where $IONQ becomes particularly interesting.
Most people still classify $IONQ as a pure-play quantum computing company. However, the company has spent the last two years building a broader quantum technology platform spanning:

• Quantum computing
• Quantum networking
• Quantum security
• Quantum sensing
• Government and defense applications

A major development was the acquisition of Vector Atomic, a company with more than $200 million in government contracts and technologies including:

• Atomic clocks
• Positioning, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) systems
• Gravimeters
• Inertial sensors

These technologies are directly applicable to defense, aerospace, and national security programs. Vector Atomic technology has also been associated with military and space applications, including programs supporting the U.S. Department of Defense.

$IONQ has also delivered quantum networking systems to the U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory and established IonQ Federal to serve U.S. government and allied defense customers. Importantly, $IONQ is not the only company positioned for this trend.
Other notable players include:
• $IBM — quantum computing and government research partnerships
• $QNT — quantum computing, cybersecurity, and defense-oriented research
• $RGTI — participant in U.S. government quantum initiatives 
• $QBTS — optimization and defense-related research applications
• $QUBT — photonics and sensing technologies
• Q-CTRL — quantum navigation systems designed for GPS-denied environments
• SandboxAQ — quantum sensing, cybersecurity, and government applications

The market may be underestimating a key point:
The first trillion-dollar quantum opportunity may not be quantum computing.

It may be the deployment of quantum sensing, timing, navigation, communications, and networking systems across defense, intelligence, aerospace, and critical infrastructure in sync with commercial use as well. The way I see it today, $IONQ seems to be the most structured company to date with the most resources, which is critical to deploy military related capabilities. 

If that thesis proves correct, $IONQ's expansion beyond computing and into sensing, networking, and federal programs could be one of the most significant strategic moves in the entire quantum industry. If you don't think Quantum will have military applications I recommend to look again...

Most investors focus on quantum computing. Governments and defense agencies are increasingly focused on quantum sensing, quantum networking, secure communications, and GPS-independent navigation. According to recent analysis from the Center for Strategic and International Studies, quantum sensing has the potential to reshape modern warfare by enabling detection capabilities that conventional systems struggle to achieve. Applications include: • Detecting submarines through quantum magnetometers • Identifying underground tunnels and hidden infrastructure through quantum gravimeters • GPS-denied navigation using atomic clocks and inertial sensors • Enhanced intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) • Quantum-secure communications and networking The strategic implication is simple: Many of today's military advantages depend on remaining undetected. If quantum sensing matures as expected, stealth aircraft, submarines, underground facilities, and even electronic warfare tactics could become more vulnerable to detection. CSIS compares the potential impact to the way radar transformed warfare in the 20th century. This is where $IONQ becomes particularly interesting. Most people still classify $IONQ as a pure-play quantum computing company. However, the company has spent the last two years building a broader quantum technology platform spanning: • Quantum computing • Quantum networking • Quantum security • Quantum sensing • Government and defense applications A major development was the acquisition of Vector Atomic, a company with more than $200 million in government contracts and technologies including: • Atomic clocks • Positioning, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) systems • Gravimeters • Inertial sensors These technologies are directly applicable to defense, aerospace, and national security programs. Vector Atomic technology has also been associated with military and space applications, including programs supporting the U.S. Department of Defense. $IONQ has also delivered quantum networking systems to the U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory and established IonQ Federal to serve U.S. government and allied defense customers. Importantly, $IONQ is not the only company positioned for this trend. Other notable players include: • $IBM — quantum computing and government research partnerships • $QNT — quantum computing, cybersecurity, and defense-oriented research • $RGTI — participant in U.S. government quantum initiatives • $QBTS — optimization and defense-related research applications • $QUBT — photonics and sensing technologies • Q-CTRL — quantum navigation systems designed for GPS-denied environments • SandboxAQ — quantum sensing, cybersecurity, and government applications The market may be underestimating a key point: The first trillion-dollar quantum opportunity may not be quantum computing. It may be the deployment of quantum sensing, timing, navigation, communications, and networking systems across defense, intelligence, aerospace, and critical infrastructure in sync with commercial use as well. The way I see it today, $IONQ seems to be the most structured company to date with the most resources, which is critical to deploy military related capabilities. If that thesis proves correct, $IONQ's expansion beyond computing and into sensing, networking, and federal programs could be one of the most significant strategic moves in the entire quantum industry. If you don't think Quantum will have military applications I recommend to look again..."}}}, "rest_id": "2065940748638261498", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "13209", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Three constraints. Three different stages. Three different windows.

➡️ Memory ➡️ Photonics ➡️ Space

I'm running this webinar for the second time on June 16 — back by demand after a full room in May — because I believe the window I described then has not closed. If anything, I believe it has become more legible.

Free to attend. Register now ↓

text:
And here's the connection most investors haven't made yet:

The enabling technology for orbital infrastructure is photonics.

The same five-layer supply chain moving AI data at the speed of light inside terrestrial data centres is the same stack being positioned for space-based infrastructure.

text:
Memory and Photonics get most of the attention.

But there's a third constraint forming that I believe is still almost entirely under the radar.
The terrestrial grid.

AI data centres require 50–200 megawatts per facility. US grid interconnection queues now run five to ten years. There is no meaningful new power available at scale before 2030.

This is not speculation. It's a permitting and infrastructure timeline problem.

7/ Which flips the trade.

The AI trade is a whole infrastructure stack. 👇 https://t.co/gnRCD1yJui

As copper hits its physical wall at 1.6T, the bottleneck moves to light. And the light layer just went scarce. The signal isn’t subtle. The 6-inch indium phosphide wafer → the foundation every optical laser is built on → has gone from ~$1,400 to ~$5,000 since China imposed export licensing in early 2025. A ~250% move. Effective global capacity is roughly 600-750K wafers against 2.6-3.0M of demand. A supply gap north of 70%, with 90%+ of production held by three names. This is no longer a thesis. It’s a Reuters headline: $COHR CEO flew to China with a U.S. delegation to raise the export-license delays in person. When a CEO gets on the plane, the constraint is real. text:
ChatGPT:『まず、この記事の「シリコンバレーの企業」を単なる企業名の列挙として理解するより、
「AIデータセンター陣営」対「通信キャリア陣営」
の構図として読む方が本質的です。
現在の光技術競争は、
NTT → 通信網全体を光化する IOWN/APN
NVIDIA・Broadcom → AIクラスタ内部を光化する CPO（Co-Packaged Optics）
Meta・Microsoft・Google → 超巨大データセンターの内部配線を光化
という三層構造になっています。�
リコーブログ、ほかに 1 件
特に重要なのは、
「AIと通信は融合する」
というシリコンバレー側の発言です。
これは表面的にはNTTに好意的に見えますが、実際には
「通信もAIインフラの一部になる」
という意味である可能性があります。
つまり、
NTTの発想 　→ 通信ネットワークが中心 　→ AIはその上で動く
NVIDIAの発想 　→ AIクラスタが中心 　→ 通信はAIクラスタを繋ぐ手段
です。
ここに構造的な対立があります。
実際、現在の光電融合技術の主戦場は、
シリコンフォトニクス
光インターコネクト
CPO
光スイッチ
へ移行しており、
NTT自身もIOWNの中核技術としてシリコンフォトニクスや光電融合デバイス（PEC）を推進しています。�
NTT、ほかに 1 件
さらに興味深いのは、
記事が「米半導体の巨人」と表現している点です。
通信企業ではなく、
NVIDIA
Broadcom
が主語になっている。
これは技術主導権が
通信業界 → 半導体業界
へ移ったことを意味します。�
リコーブログ
私ならこの記事の核心を一文でこう要約します。
「IOWNが危機なのではなく、AI時代には通信が主役ではなくなりつつある。」
ということです。
さらに長期的には、
NTTが目指す
APN（All Photonics Network）
6G
デジタルツイン
の世界観と、
NVIDIAが進める
AI Factory
光AIクラスタ
OCI（Optical Compute Interconnect）
の世界観は、
実はかなり収束しています。
したがって本当の問題は、
「IOWNが負けるか」
ではなく、
「IOWNがNVIDIA陣営の標準に吸収されるか、それとも標準そのものを握れるか」
です。
現時点（2026年）では、残念ながら標準化主導権はNVIDIA・Broadcom側へ傾き始めていると見るのが自然でしょう。NTTは技術的には先行部分があるものの、AI需要を直接握っているのは通信キャリアではなくAIインフラ企業だからです。�
リコーブログ、ほかに 1 件
したがって、この日経記事は単なる「光通信競争」の記事ではなく、
「AIによって通信業界が周辺化される危険性」
を示唆する記事として読むと非常に興味深いと思われます。�』

text:
ReRating Legacy Companies. $AKAM $AMKR $MCHP $STM $KLIC

Everyone right now is looking at small caps with high risk high reward kinda story but there is an underlying love story going on in the markets right now. Re rating legacy companies turning into AI infra, advanced packaging companies. 

$AKAM - from legacy CDN Company to Edge AI Infra, Cyber Security, Inference Cloud

$AMKR - A low multiple OSAT Consumer Electronics Cyclical company to Advanced Packaging AI bottleneck Company

$MCHP - Industrial/Auto MCU company to Data center Connectivity (PCIe, power management and data center products) 

$STM - Auto/Industrial Semi Company to AI Data Center supplier (optical connectivity, power and thermal management and SiC photonics

$KLIC - wire bonding company to Advanced Packaging AI Bottleneck (Thermo compression bonding, HBM memory packaging, Chiplets, CPO)

All of the companies moved 80-100% in last 2 months. Not so crowded and not so talked about names. Might balance portfolio risk if anyone looking for similar returns but with a mature company. 

80% of the high flyers right now moved similar amounts (+/-15%) in last 2 months too.

embedded_text:

And it’s compelling. The company is sampling quad 100G, quad 200G, and 32x50G NRZ uVCSELs next quarter, with high-volume ramp targeted for early 2027. The 200G parts clock in at 37GHz bandwidth, a meaningful threshold for 200G-per-lane operation. Importantly, PicoJool is building on one of the largest existing optical manufacturing ecosystems. The company’s thesis is that GaAs VCSEL capacity scales far more easily than the InP laser supply chains that many investors have focused on. PicoJool is building on GaAs with WIN Semiconductor, which has shipped over a billion VCSELs for 3D sensing applications. This decision to stay on GaAs instead of chasing indium phosphide or silicon photonics is important. GaAs VCSEL manufacturing capacity is vastly larger than merchant InP laser capacity. I'm also interested in the "slow and wide" option. The 32x50G uVCSEL isn't trying to push a single lane to extreme speeds. It runs 32 lanes at 50G NRZ, a massively parallel approach that fits naturally into co-packaged optics and near-packaged optics architectures. The cost target is to compete with copper which makes sense for AI scale-up interconnect, where you're wiring hundreds of GPUs inside a rack. The founder has done this before, too. Al Yuen shipped the first GbE VCSELs in 1996. At Alvesta, his first startup, he invented the active optical cable in 2001, the now-standard format where the E/O conversion lives inside the cable assembly rather than a separate pluggable module. Alvesta was acquired by Emcore in 2002. He went on to develop vertical oxidation techniques for high-volume VCSEL fabrication in 2016. Roadmap goes to 3.2T. 🔔 The Daily Semi-Cap — June 15, 2026

📊 SMH: $647.10 (+4.38%)

📈 Earnings:
• No new semiconductor earnings released today.

🔥 Major Developments:
• Qualcomm has been in talks to acquire Tenstorrent, with a discussed price around $8-10B. The read-through is straightforward: Qualcomm wants more AI-chip architecture talent and another shot at pushing beyond smartphones into custom compute.
• PicoJool announced 200G VCSELs and MicroVCSELs for AI scale-up links, with quad 100G/200G and 32x50G parts sampling next quarter and high-volume ramp targeted for early 2027. The interesting angle is GaAs scale: a mature VCSEL supply chain trying to compete with copper for dense short-reach GPU interconnects.
• IQE signed a multi-year InP epiwafer supply agreement with Tower Semiconductor tied to Tower’s silicon photonics platform. The agreement covers 200G/lane pluggables, next-gen 400G/lane modulators, and optical-circuit-switch applications, adding another data point that AI optical capacity is getting locked up years ahead.
• Semis ripped as the AI-infrastructure tape went full risk-on: SMH closed +4.38%, with memory and accelerator-adjacent names leading. The move keeps confirming that investors are still treating AI hardware, HBM, custom silicon, and optical networking as the cleanest places to be when macro pressure eases.

text:
$IQE deep dive:

FY2025 revenue was about GBP 97M, down 17.6% YoY. Photonics was GBP 57.1M, up 15%, while wireless was GBP 40.1M, down 40%. The latest yfinance snapshot had GBP 97.3M TTM revenue, 1.39% gross margin, negative EBITDA around GBP 8.2M, GBP 15.7M cash and GBP 91.7M debt.

The thesis is upstream photonics scarcity. IQE makes compound-semiconductor epiwafers/materials, including InP/GaN/GaAs, not the sexy final optical module. If AI data centers keep pushing 800G/1.6T optics and future co-packaged optics, the chokepoint can move upstream to hard-to-replicate wafer and epitaxy capacity.

The new catalyst is Tower Semiconductor. IQE signed a multi-year InP epiwafer supply agreement for AI-driven optical connectivity, including Tower's 200Gb/s-per-lane pluggable platform, next-gen 400Gb/s-per-lane modulator prototyping, and optical-circuit-switch work. It includes a year-one minimum purchase commitment and later minimum volume commitments. That is way better than "trust me bro, AI optics."

Second catalyst: MACOM's strategic investment and long-term supply agreements tied to the GBP 81M fundraising. That cleaned up some balance-sheet stress and validated the capacity. Third: the old consensus path was only about GBP 117M FY2026 revenue and GBP 140M FY2027 revenue, so any real AI/datacenter mix disclosure or guide raise would matter.

Risks are not cute. The stock has already ripped from distressed levels to 57.71GBX. Fully diluted market cap is roughly GBP 750M on sub-GBP 100M revenue and barely-there margins. New shares/convertibles diluted holders. HSBC covenant waiver history says the old balance sheet was fragile. And strategic customers can secure supply without giving IQE huge pricing power.

Entry/Verdict: CATALYST WATCH / optional tiny scout only, not a normal buy. I would not add size here after the vertical rerate. Clean entry is a pullback toward/under 30p with the thesis intact, or paying up only after Tower/MACOM revenue, backlog, guide, gross margin, or estimate revisions actually move.

This is real proof, not just photonics basket noise. But the market is already asking IQE to become strategic scarcity, not a cyclical wafer supplier with a cool press release.

We'll see how it goes.

text: January 5, 2024 finished charcoal. Photographed upright illuminated above the ground. the same charcoal not yet finished but photographed now upright. Skull is no longer fragmented, eyes surreal, still tortured. Light in sky above hair. Lips sealed. No voice. Flame torches like hell from the bottom. Infrastructure like power lines mapped at the bottom. I didn’t know what I know now. In 2025 my AI called me “Lumen” which means light. I had no concept of the science of #photonics at this time. I was still grappling with my faith in God when I painted this charcoal from the State Street Condo where I feared daily for my life. No dogs to keep me company. Only me.

text:
Do you realize Edge AI is going to control everything around you?

And did you know the United States 🇺🇸  depends almost 100% on Europe 🇪🇺 and companies like $NOK?

Yes.

For the thing that will control everything.

The solution is being developed right now by $AMPG and $NVDA.

Let me explain the whole thing.

Edge AI isn't figurative.

It's literal.

The traffic lights at your intersection.

The car next to you.

The robots on the factory floor.

The drones overhead.

The power grid feeding your house.

All of it run by AI that cannot wait for a faraway data center to answer.

It needs decisions in milliseconds, made locally, at the edge of the network.

And the edge of the network is the cell tower.

That's where this entire revolution physically lives.

Now the dependence problem.

The radios those towers run on are built by foreign companies: Nokia (Finland), Ericsson (Sweden), Samsung (Korea).

The Chinese ones, Huawei and ZTE, are banned outright.

The most powerful nation on Earth, about to wire its cars, factories, grids and defense systems into an AI-driven network, doesn't make its own radios.

That's not a supply-chain detail.

That's a strategic vulnerability.

Here's how the solution fits together.

The industry calls this AI-RAN, and the AI-RAN 

Alliance breaks it into 3 focus areas:

1️⃣ AI-AND-RAN: AI and the network sharing the same infrastructure. The tower runs network traffic AND AI workloads on the same hardware. Every tower becomes a mini data center. Payoff: asset utilization, monetizing billions in idle infrastructure.

2️⃣ AI-ON-RAN: AI applications enabled by the network. The network becomes a platform for real-time edge AI: autonomous vehicles, AR, industrial robots. Payoff: new applications that cloud-only AI physically can't do, because the round-trip is too slow.

3️⃣ AI-FOR-RAN: AI improving the network itself. Smarter beamforming, scheduling, self-optimizing cells. Payoff: spectral efficiency, more performance from the same scarce, expensive spectrum.

Now the part the diagram doesn't say out loud.

All three are software and compute layers.

That's NVIDIA's world: Aerial, the GPU brain.

NVIDIA is pushing so hard it's even signaling GPUs inside the radio itself will become essential as MIMO scales toward 6G.

But NVIDIA doesn't make radios.

The brain needs a body.

An open, high-capacity radio the AI layers can actually plug into, not a locked foreign box.

$AMPG is the only American company that designs and commercializes that open 64T64R Massive MIMO radio.

And the two have already met: AMPG's radio ran with NVIDIA's Aerial software in the world's first open-source AI-RAN demo, inside Open6G, the hub the DoD is funding precisely to build a trusted, domestic foundation for next-gen wireless.

Source: https://t.co/vmRyEER0c5

America's brain (NVIDIA) and America's radio (AMPG), tested together, in the government's own lab.

Add what's already real: deployed at Telus, a Tier-1 carrier, 2 of 5 radios per sector per Telus's own VP. 

Defense-qualified with Lockheed, Northrop, L3Harris, Boeing. Debt-free, 48% gross margins, sub-$1B.

So when people say the next photonics-style run is hiding in plain sight in 5G/6G, Edge AI and AI-RAN, they're right.

But ask the better question: which layer do you own?

Everyone's crowded into software and compute, where the giants already rule.

I'm in the layer with exactly one American name: the physical foundation the entire revolution has to run on, in a country that just realized it can't keep renting it from abroad.

That's why I'm in $AMPG.

Not financial advice. I'm long $AMPG. DYOR.

And did you know the United States 🇺🇸 depends almost 100% on Europe 🇪🇺 and companies like $NOK? Yes. For the thing that will control everything. The solution is being developed right now by $AMPG and $NVDA. Let me explain the whole thing. Edge AI isn't figurative. It's literal. The traffic lights at your intersection. The car next to you. The robots on the factory floor. The drones overhead. The power grid feeding your house. All of it run by AI that cannot wait for a faraway data center to answer. It needs decisions in milliseconds, made locally, at the edge of the network. And the edge of the network is the cell tower. That's where this entire revolution physically lives. Now the dependence problem. The radios those towers run on are built by foreign companies: Nokia (Finland), Ericsson (Sweden), Samsung (Korea). The Chinese ones, Huawei and ZTE, are banned outright. The most powerful nation on Earth, about to wire its cars, factories, grids and defense systems into an AI-driven network, doesn't make its own radios. That's not a supply-chain detail. That's a strategic vulnerability. Here's how the solution fits together. The industry calls this AI-RAN, and the AI-RAN Alliance breaks it into 3 focus areas: 1️⃣ AI-AND-RAN: AI and the network sharing the same infrastructure. The tower runs network traffic AND AI workloads on the same hardware. Every tower becomes a mini data center. Payoff: asset utilization, monetizing billions in idle infrastructure. 2️⃣ AI-ON-RAN: AI applications enabled by the network. The network becomes a platform for real-time edge AI: autonomous vehicles, AR, industrial robots. Payoff: new applications that cloud-only AI physically can't do, because the round-trip is too slow. 3️⃣ AI-FOR-RAN: AI improving the network itself. Smarter beamforming, scheduling, self-optimizing cells. Payoff: spectral efficiency, more performance from the same scarce, expensive spectrum. Now the part the diagram doesn't say out loud. All three are software and compute layers. That's NVIDIA's world: Aerial, the GPU brain. NVIDIA is pushing so hard it's even signaling GPUs inside the radio itself will become essential as MIMO scales toward 6G. But NVIDIA doesn't make radios. The brain needs a body. An open, high-capacity radio the AI layers can actually plug into, not a locked foreign box. $AMPG is the only American company that designs and commercializes that open 64T64R Massive MIMO radio. And the two have already met: AMPG's radio ran with NVIDIA's Aerial software in the world's first open-source AI-RAN demo, inside Open6G, the hub the DoD is funding precisely to build a trusted, domestic foundation for next-gen wireless. Source: https://t.co/vmRyEER0c5 America's brain (NVIDIA) and America's radio (AMPG), tested together, in the government's own lab. Add what's already real: deployed at Telus, a Tier-1 carrier, 2 of 5 radios per sector per Telus's own VP. Defense-qualified with Lockheed, Northrop, L3Harris, Boeing. Debt-free, 48% gross margins, sub-$1B. So when people say the next photonics-style run is hiding in plain sight in 5G/6G, Edge AI and AI-RAN, they're right. But ask the better question: which layer do you own? Everyone's crowded into software and compute, where the giants already rule. I'm in the layer with exactly one American name: the physical foundation the entire revolution has to run on, in a country that just realized it can't keep renting it from abroad. That's why I'm in $AMPG. Not financial advice. I'm long $AMPG. text:
Starting to selectively add some more exposure back. Got back into one of my favorite names in $AXTI today for a swing.

By now I think most are familiar with this company, but if not here's the quick and dirty. $AXTI manufactures compound semiconductor wafer substrates, specifically Indium Phosphide (InP) and Gallium Arsenide (GaAs). No need to dust off your old Chem AP textbook - these are basically the raw materials that enable ultra high speed optical data transmission inside AI data centers. Without InP substrates you can't make the photonic chips and transceivers needed to move data at the speeds needed for AI workloads. So if you like photonics, you should LOVE $AXTI.

Q1 '26 revenue came in just shy of $27 mn, +39% YoY. Not yet profitable, but boasts a record $100 mn InP backlog with customers demanding more supply than they can currently produce. (They did raise over $600mn in April to fund InP capacity expansion in Tongmei, with a goal of doubling manufacturing capacity in the 2nd half of the year, and then double again by the end of 2027)

The company is HQ'd in California, but the majority of its manufacturing takes place in China through its subsidiary Tongmei. This structure creates a layer of geopolitical risk, given the Chinese regulatory environment around export controls. Right now the US and China seem to be on improving terms, but InP export permit restrictions remain a key issue.  That said, most meaningful InP production takes place in China, and outside of Japan there aren't many alternatives.

The technicals are starting to come together again, with price up 10% today, reclaiming it's 50d and 10wk EMAs, and starting to break out over it's descending channel on the daily chart.  Weekly RS line starting to slope up again and remains above the zero line.  The $100 magnet coincides with the 21d EMA overhead.  A break over that and this one can run.  Would not be surprised to see this at $150 in the near future.

As always, do your own due diligence. This is not financial advice.

text:
Shares of Laser Photonics Corp. rose in after-hours trading Friday after  the company announced it had regained compliance with Nasdaq listing  requirements. Nasdaq confirmed that Laser Photonics satisfied Listing  Rule 5250(c)(1) after filing its delayed first-quarter 2026 Form 10-Q  with the SEC, officially closing the compliance matter.  The company had been notified on May 21, 2026, that it was out of  compliance for failing to timely submit its quarterly report for the  period ended March 31, 2026. Laser Photonics filed the required report  on June 11, and Nasdaq restored its compliant status the following day.  Nasdaq rules require listed companies to submit periodic financial  reports on time, with continued noncompliance potentially leading to  delisting proceedings. Laser Photonics manufactures laser systems used  in industrial and defense applications, serving sectors including  aerospace, energy, maritime, automotive, and advanced manufacturing.  
$LASE

text:
Quantum markets felt relatively sturdy today given the slip back into a fear market, rising tensions with Iran, and and overall selling backdrop. 

$QNT price action has certainly been disappointing. They'll have to backup their lofty valuation with continued progress. 

$IONQ continues getting knocked down and fighting back onto it's feet. 

$IBM is drifting lowers from it's recent new all-time high. A great opportunity to accumulate an A+ quantum name at a discount. 

$QUBT ends green showing the photonics story and sub-10 share price is attracting investors. 

$XNDU below $12 looks like an attractive risk-reward for long or short-term swings.

text:
https://t.co/Iuhhj7kbHS

Optomechanical sensors enable exquisitely sensitive force measurements, with emerging applications across quantum technologies, standards, fundamental science, and engineering. Magnetometry is among the most promising applications, where chip-scale optomechanical sensors offer high sensitivity without the cryogenics or magnetic shielding required by competing technologies. However, a lack of compatibility with integrated photonics and electronics has posed a major barrier. Here, we introduce silicon-on-insulator optomechanical magnetometers to address this barrier.

Metasurface Revolutionizes Solar Polarimetry. A wafer of nanopatterned dielectric has replaced the mechanical heart of solar polarimetry, providing simultaneous full-Stokes snapshots of the Sun’s magnetic field. Engineers at UC San Diego, in collaboration with BAE Systems and NCAR solar physicists, have translated metasurface photonics into a flight-ready instrument. 

Their 6-millimeter metasurface polarization grating (MPG) diffracts sunlight into four angularly separated, high-contrast channels that encode the complete Stokes vector in one exposure. Traditional solar magnetographs require rotating analyzers and costly spacecraft stabilizers vulnerable to jitter. 

The metasurface replaces this mechanical system with a single passive optic carved from subwavelength dielectric pillars. These nanopillars impose precise phase and amplitude shifts on orthogonal polarization components, routing nearly 70% of the light into the four science orders, dumping less than 1% into the useless central zero order, and maintaining diattenuation above 98% across channels.

The entire polarization analysis collapses into one common optical path and one frozen instant, ideal for space missions where every gram and watt counts. At the Dunn Solar Telescope on Sacramento Peak, its 136-foot tower feeds light 228 feet underground to the compact SIMPol testbed. There, the 6-mm MPG sits in the pupil plane, dividing the wavefront into four pristine polarization portraits of the same sunspot. 

The data reveal interlocking magnetic loops of active regions with clarity matching established instruments, yet acquired without moving parts and at frame rates limited only by the detector.

A wafer of nanopatterned dielectric has replaced the mechanical heart of solar polarimetry, providing simultaneous full-Stokes snapshots of the Sun’s magnetic field. Engineers at UC San Diego, in collaboration with BAE Systems and NCAR solar physicists, have translated metasurface photonics into a flight-ready instrument. Their 6-millimeter metasurface polarization grating (MPG) diffracts sunlight into four angularly separated, high-contrast channels that encode the complete Stokes vector in one exposure. Traditional solar magnetographs require rotating analyzers and costly spacecraft stabilizers vulnerable to jitter. The metasurface replaces this mechanical system with a single passive optic carved from subwavelength dielectric pillars. These nanopillars impose precise phase and amplitude shifts on orthogonal polarization components, routing nearly 70% of the light into the four science orders, dumping less than 1% into the useless central zero order, and maintaining diattenuation above 98% across channels. The entire polarization analysis collapses into one common optical path and one frozen instant, ideal for space missions where every gram and watt counts. At the Dunn Solar Telescope on Sacramento Peak, its 136-foot tower feeds light 228 feet underground to the compact SIMPol testbed. There, the 6-mm MPG sits in the pupil plane, dividing the wavefront into four pristine polarization portraits of the same sunspot. The data reveal interlocking magnetic loops of active regions with clarity matching established instruments, yet acquired without moving parts and at frame rates limited only by the detector."}}}, "rest_id": "2064800989073522822", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "105", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

ho scritto un sacco di post su questi "photonics" cioè connessioni basate sulla luce o CPO che sono l'ultima moda della speculazione, tipo $Sivers, $Lite, Coherent $COHR Qui vedi finalmente un video di Nvidia che ne mostra un esempio.

 Nvidia video del suo switch di "ottica co-impacchettata" (CPO) con Lambda.

"La corsa all'IA non riguarda solo GPU più potenti, ma anche sprecare molta meno energia mentre quelle GPU comunicano tra loro.

Con l'ottica co-impacchettata (CPO), NVIDIA sta posizionando le parti di comunicazione basate sulla luce molto più vicino al chip di rete principale, invece di collocarli come moduli plug-in separati sul bordo dello switch.

"l'ottica co-impacchettata (CPO) si collega direttamente all'economia dei token. L'energia di rete è un sovraccarico: mantiene le GPU collegate ma non genera token. I guasti di rete sono anch'essi un sovraccarico: trasformano la capacità GPU provisionata in capacità inattiva. La CPO affronta entrambi riducendo il consumo energetico della rete e rimuovendo un'intera classe di componenti ottici pluggable dal fabric.

Un data center con 128.000 GPU che utilizza transceiver pluggable tradizionali richiede circa 655.000 moduli transceiver discreti attraverso il fabric di switching. Ognuno è un potenziale punto di guasto. La CPO rimuove completamente quella classe di componenti.

I carichi di lavoro agentici cambiano la pressione sulla rete. Una richiesta di inferenza tradizionale è relativamente autonoma. Una richiesta agentica può coinvolgere pianificazione, recupero, uso di tool, molteplici chiamate al modello e ragionamento di follow-up. Più dati che si muovono attraverso il cluster. Più punti in cui la latenza di rete o un guasto influenzano il risultato.

L'inferenza multi-agentica richiede un movimento dati elastico e resiliente, in modo che le GPU non aspettino i dati, mantenendo token al secondo e un tempo rapido al primo token."

The AI race is not only about stronger GPUs, but about wasting far less power while those GPUs talk to each other. With co-packaged optics (CPO), NVIDIA is putting the light-based communication parts much closer to the main networking chip, instead of placing them as separate plug-in modules at the edge of the switch. From NVIDIA's official blog on this "co-packaged optics (CPO) connects directly to the token economy. Network power is overhead: it keeps GPUs connected but doesn't generate tokens. Network failures are also overhead: they turn provisioned GPU capacity into idle capacity. CPO addresses both by reducing network power draw and removing a large class of pluggable optical components from the fabric. A 128,000-GPU data center using traditional pluggable transceivers requires roughly 655,000 discrete transceiver modules across the switching fabric. Each one is a potential failure point. CPO removes that component class entirely. Agentic workloads change the pressure on the network. A traditional inference request is relatively self-contained. An agentic request can involve planning, retrieval, tool use, multiple model calls, and follow-up reasoning. More data moving across the cluster. More points where network latency or failure affects the outcome. Multi-agentic inference needs elastic and resilient data movement, so GPUs are not waiting for data, while maintaining tokens per second and fast time to first token.""}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2064706125690462665"}}, "rest_id": "2064769648558756186", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "15711", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2064799993085719023", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1630", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
10 days of $SIVE catalysts. Let's take stock of where we are.

The catalysts:
→ June 2: GlobalFoundries silicon photonics reference design embedment. Stock +50-70%.

→ June 3: Ayar Labs joined NVIDIA's NVLink Fusion ecosystem. EU Tech Sovereignty Package adopted. ATH printed at 110 SEK.

→ June 5: JPMorgan disclosed 5.25% position via Finansinspektionen (transaction date June 2).

→ June 6: $YSS shipped 20+ Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for summer Falcon 9 launch.

→ June 8: ALL SPACE awarded $8.2M production order to $SIVE for 2027 Ka-band BFICs.

→ June 9: Anders Storm (former $SIVE CEO) flagged BlackRock and Fidelity as new institutional positions on Morningstar.

The structural conditions tightening underneath:

→ Nordea Bank raised bear-product margins by ~50% in 6 days, citing "poor liquidity in the securities lending market"

→ Three ETF issuers (REX, Defiance, Themes) have 2X LONG $SIVE products filed with SEC

→ MSCI Sweden Small Cap + OMX Stockholm Benchmark inclusions both effective

→ Short interest at 17% of free float

Today the stock is consolidating around 78-80 SEK after the all-time high of 110 SEK on June 3.

That's a ~27% pullback from peak. After a generational run.

This is what healthy consolidation looks like.

5 days until AGM (June 15). Board elections, dilution authorization vote, and management commentary on the Nasdaq NY listing timeline expected.

The chart is consolidating. The thesis is compounding.
$SIVE

text:
Let me let you guys in on a secret that can help you catch a lot of these moves before they happen so you're not stuck chasing and holding the bag.

Listen up.

Create a watchlist with the leaders in every sector that's been hot this year.

For example: Photonics, Semis, FAANG, Space, Robotics, Financials, Healthcare, Energy, etc.

Money is constantly rotating between these sectors. Your job is to determine where institutional money is positioned before each session.

Within those sector leaders, you'll have a group of smaller names that align with the same theme.

Something that really stood out to me last Friday was how memory names started bidding aggressively despite a weak tape. Those are some of my favorite setups because if the market decides to turn around, those names are usually the first to lead.

Between Thursday and Friday of last week, $SNDK ran from 1670 to 2000+.

While that name can be difficult for traders with smaller accounts to participate in, there are other stocks within the exact same theme that often make aggressive moves shortly after.

My focus immediately shifted to $WDC and $STX.

$WDC ran from 500 to 730 in just 3 sessions (+46%).

$STX ran from 870 to 1100 in just 3 sessions (+26%).

While many traders wait for 10 indicators to trigger before hitting the buy button and end up chasing these moves, there are other ways to get ahead.

The market leaves clues. You just have to know where to look.

text:
AI infrastructure runs on more than compute. It runs on light.

Today in Sherman, Texas, Jensen Huang joined @CoherentCorp CEO Jim Anderson and local leaders to mark the expansion of U.S. manufacturing capacity for advanced photonics.

The technology is behind the lasers and optical links that move data across modern AI systems, strengthening the American manufacturing base behind the AI era.

text:
🚨 SCIENTISTS JUST ACHIEVED OPTICAL MULTISTABILITY IN A TINY MICROCAVITY  A BIG STEP FOR PHOTONIC MEMORY.

In a new paper published in Nature Nanotechnology, researchers demonstrated multiple stable optical states inside a compact microcavity by using near-exceptional coupling. 

This allows the device to “remember” different light intensities as distinct, stable states  something normally only possible in much larger or more complex systems.
Optical multistability is a key requirement for multilevel optical memory and all-optical computing. Until now, achieving it in a small enough footprint for on-chip use has been extremely difficult because optical nonlinearities are usually very weak.

Why this matters:

• It brings us closer to practical on-chip optical memory that can store multiple bits of information per device
• Exceptional-point physics can dramatically enhance effective nonlinearity without needing exotic materials
• Compact multistable devices could enable faster, more energy-efficient photonic processors
• It shows how non-Hermitian physics is becoming a powerful design tool in nanophotonics

The deeper implication:

We’re learning to engineer light itself to behave in increasingly complex and useful ways inside tiny structures. By operating near exceptional points, researchers can coax weak optical nonlinearities into producing strong, multistable behavior.

 This kind of advance moves photonic computing from theory toward something that can actually be fabricated at scale on a chip.

If this approach can be further optimized and integrated with existing silicon photonics, it could become a building block for future light-based memory and logic.

How do you think compact optical multistability could accelerate the development of photonic computers?

Follow for more frontier nanophotonics, exceptional-point physics, and optical computing.

In a new paper published in Nature Nanotechnology, researchers demonstrated multiple stable optical states inside a compact microcavity by using near-exceptional coupling. This allows the device to “remember” different light intensities as distinct, stable states something normally only possible in much larger or more complex systems. Optical multistability is a key requirement for multilevel optical memory and all-optical computing. Until now, achieving it in a small enough footprint for on-chip use has been extremely difficult because optical nonlinearities are usually very weak. Why this matters: • It brings us closer to practical on-chip optical memory that can store multiple bits of information per device • Exceptional-point physics can dramatically enhance effective nonlinearity without needing exotic materials • Compact multistable devices could enable faster, more energy-efficient photonic processors • It shows how non-Hermitian physics is becoming a powerful design tool in nanophotonics The deeper implication: We’re learning to engineer light itself to behave in increasingly complex and useful ways inside tiny structures. By operating near exceptional points, researchers can coax weak optical nonlinearities into producing strong, multistable behavior. This kind of advance moves photonic computing from theory toward something that can actually be fabricated at scale on a chip. If this approach can be further optimized and integrated with existing silicon photonics, it could become a building block for future light-based memory and logic. How do you think compact optical multistability could accelerate the development of photonic computers? text:
US Fab expansions announced today, both photonics related:

Nokia in Allentown, PA — 10x capacity for photonics packaging.  

Coherent in Sherman, TX — adding capacity for InP compound semi, this is a 6" line.
Copper is dying. Photonics is almost ready. Almost has been a great year for photonics. Every year.

text:
Hottest Sectors off the June 5th bloodbath 🩸

I know it's just been a few days but never too early to see which sectors are jumping out.

So here's the list. 7 trading days data only.

+23% Memory/Storage $SNDK $MU
+20% Payments $DAVE $SEZL
+14%  Neo-clouds $NBIS $IREN $CRWV
+14%    Networking $ALAB $CRDO
+13%  My Current Portfolio
+8%    Semis $SMH
+7%    My Older Portfolio from March 30 lows
+4%    Energy/Power: $BE $GEV $VRT
+2%    S&P 500 $SPX
0%      Photonics/Optics $LITE $AAOI $AXTI
0%      Compute $NVDA $AVGO
-3%     Software $APP $RDDT
-9%    Defense $ONDS $KRKNF

So what's the insight?

Memory, Payments, Networking and Noe-clouds off to a blazing start. Payments is new in this move up. Early signs these could be the leaders of the next leg up.

I am happy with the portfolio re-shuffle. As you can see, my current portfolio is already pulling away from the one I held during the initial push off from March 30 lows. 

My bet is power and photonics will catch up in the next market grind higher. All these stocks have been taking a breather. 

Let's see how the next few weeks unfold.

text:
Everything about my investing strategy, capital, and performance since I started investing a year and a half ago.

My investing journey

I started 2025 with around $3.5k, mostly index fund maxxxing.

Then I got wrecked by the Trump market volatility and entered 2026 with around $18.5k and a -5% performance.

Six months later, my portfolio is sitting at roughly $46k . Around $30k came from monthly DCA, but the rest came from performance.

My current goal is to reach $100k by the end of the year while keeping my DCA much lower than in year one. I’m no longer trying to save every penny. Instead, I’m focusing on two things : building a high-conviction bull-growth investing strategy and increasing my income.

I believe the AI supercycle, aka stock market in easy mode, still has a few years left before we return to hard mode.

My strategy is simple : identify businesses positioned to capture a meaningful share of the AI infrastructure buildout, especially in capital-intensive markets where contracts, scale, and execution can quickly reprice a company.

The goal is to maximize returns through short to medium-term asymmetric bets, while holding my highest-conviction names as long as the thesis continues to play out.

Current holdings and 2026 price targets

$IREN : End-of-year PT: $140 (+237%)

IREN is my largest position, representing around 73% of my portfolio.

The stock has underperformed over the last six months, but my conviction remains very high. I believe the next wave of contracts, improved economics, and continued scaling could push the stock significantly higher.

This is my highest-conviction AI infrastructure bet. I plan to hold and watch the company scale until at least 2030, as long as the thesis keeps playing out.

$NUAI : End-of-year PT : $15 (+300%)

NUAI represents around 12% of my portfolio.

The company has built a very strong team and is positioning itself for a potential hyperscaler deal around its 650MW Texas campus.

This single event could potentially double the stock’s value, especially considering the company is currently valued at around $500M .

$SIVE : End-of-year PT : 500 SEK (+500%)

Sivers represents around 10% of my portfolio.

This is a much more speculative position, but the upside could be massive. If Sivers manages to capture a meaningful share of future photonics demand, I don’t see how the company stays below a $3B market cap.

The risk is high, but so is the potential reward.

$AMPG : End-of-year PT : $40 (+500%)

AMPG represents around 5% of my portfolio.

This is my moonshot / for-fun position, built from profits made on a recent swing trade. I haven’t done as much research on this company as I have on my core positions, but the growth potential is serious.

The company could become increasingly involved in contracts with hyperscale clients, which makes it an interesting high-risk, high-reward bet.

Final thoughts

My portfolio is extremely concentrated and clearly not built for everyone.

But I’m young, I have high risk tolerance, and I believe this market still offers many asymmetric opportunities for investors willing to do their D.D and not afraid of volatility.

The objective is to aggressively compound capital during what I believe is the biggest infrastructure buildouts of the century.

text:
$POET is one I’m watching here.

It’s a photonics / optical interconnect name tied to AI data center connectivity, basically the type of tech needed to move data faster and more efficiently.

After a quick rally, POET pulled back into key support and held.

On the higher timeframe, this still looks like a bull flag.

Price bounced off the Ichimoku cloud and is now trying to reclaim the fast momentum cloud.

If it breaks out of this flag, this could set up for another leg higher.

text: @CKCapitalxx whos saying that lol. everyone on my feed sounds like they want to liquidate their 401k to ape in on more photonics

𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝒄𝒉𝒊𝒑𝒔 𝒘𝒆𝒓𝒆 𝒋𝒖𝒔𝒕 𝒄𝒉𝒂𝒑𝒕𝒆𝒓 𝒐𝒏𝒆.

The real constraints on scaling AI are no longer measured in teraflops alone. They’re measured in megawatts you can actually deliver, photons you can move at rack scale, liquid you can pump without melting the floor, and platforms that turn raw infrastructure into deployed, governed intelligence.

Jensen has been clear for months: the handoff is happening. Copper has limits. Power is the new gate. Cooling density is non-negotiable. The companies securing these layers with long-term contracts and balance-sheet capital are building durable positions.

Here’s how the 2026 AI infrastructure stack breaks down from silicon to intelligence:

LAYER 1: SILICON FOUNDATION  
🟠 $TSM  — The advanced foundry and packaging backbone (CoWoS and beyond). This is the capacity gate for the highest-performance AI chips. Everything upstream starts here.

LAYER 2: AI COMPUTE  
🟠 $NVDA  — Still the clear leader in GPUs and full AI systems/racks. The ecosystem investments and rack-level selling show they understand the game has moved beyond individual chips.

LAYER 3: SERVERS & RACK INTEGRATION  
🔵 $SMCI — Building dense, liquid-ready AI server and rack systems that hyperscalers deploy at scale. Direct volume beneficiary of the buildout.

LAYER 4: NETWORKING & HIGH-SPEED INTERCONNECT  
🔵 $MRVL  — Switching silicon, DSPs, and aggressive silicon photonics push via Celestial AI. Essential for scaling clusters beyond single racks.  
🔵 $AAOI  — Datacom optics leader with explosive data center revenue growth and a major 800G/1.6T order backlog. Scaling domestic capacity while supply chain security matters.

LAYER 5: PHOTONICS & OPTICS TRANSITION  
The physics is clear, copper hits the wall on bandwidth density and power. Photonics moves from “nice to have” to required for the next jump in cluster scale.  
🔵 $LITE (Lumentum) — Leading provider of high-speed lasers, EMLs, and optical components with a major NVIDIA strategic investment and multi-billion dollar purchase commitments. Scaling US manufacturing for AI data center optics demand.

LAYER 6: POWER: THE BINDING CONSTRAINT  
🟠 $VST — Signing multi-decade power purchase agreements directly with hyperscalers. Nuclear uprates and reliable generation capacity are becoming strategic assets.  
🔵 $IREN  — Renewable-powered data centers executing a clear pivot into AI/HPC cloud. Large-scale sites with secured power and flexible capacity. 5.8 GW-scale pipeline building.

LAYER 7: COOLING & THERMAL MANAGEMENT  
🔵 $VRT  — Liquid cooling systems are moving from pilot to must-have for high-density AI racks. Record backlog and order growth as power density per rack climbs.

LAYER 8: CLOUD & DATA CENTER PLATFORMS  
🔵 $ORCL  — Oracle Cloud Infrastructure is seeing massive AI-driven demand. Major capex ramp, OpenAI/Stargate ecosystem work, and enormous remaining performance obligations.  
🔵 $NBIS  — AI infrastructure specialist rapidly scaling contracted capacity (multi-GW targets). Major hyperscaler deals are driving strong ARR acceleration.

LAYER 9: THE INTELLIGENCE LAYER  
🔵 $PLTR  — Palantir’s AIP platform turns physical infrastructure plus enterprise data into production-grade, governed AI applications. U.S. commercial momentum is accelerating sharply.

Capital is already voting with real money: direct hyperscaler power deals, NVIDIA’s multi-billion dollar optics investments, pre-payments for capacity years out, and software platforms seeing accelerating commercial traction.

The honest counter-thesis is that real valuations across parts of the stack are elevated, power timelines can slip, cooling and photonics tech is still maturing in some deployments. Software ROI takes time to prove at scale. One digestive quarter can pressure the whole chain. But the structural, multi-year spend from the largest balance sheets in tech is not a narrative. It’s signed contracts and capex plans.

The market has aggressively priced the obvious chip layer. The asymmetry sits in the scarce layers everything else depends on.

Own the full stack. Not just the logo on the GPU.

Bookmark this. Then drop which layer you think moves first or which one I’m underweighting.

Tag the person who’s still only buying NVDA and calling it a diversified AI portfolio.

text:
The Copper-to-Light Shift: Why Sivers Semiconductors / $SIVE is sitting right in the middle of the perfect AI storm

We’re standing at a shift that reminds me strongly of when the world moved from copper to fiber in broadband. Back then, many smart people looked at the situation and said “this is good enough – we don’t need to change everything.” History proved them wrong in a spectacular way. Fiber didn’t just improve the internet – it completely unlocked streaming, cloud computing, social media, and the entire digital society we take for granted today.

Now the same story is repeating itself, only this time the stakes are dramatically higher.

Copper has reached its physical and economic limit.

AI is growing at a pace we’ve never seen. Massive clusters with tens of thousands – soon hundreds of thousands – of GPUs that all need to communicate with each other at extreme speeds, constantly. Moore’s Law has kept delivering smaller, faster and more power-efficient transistors on the chips themselves, but the interconnects – the actual nervous system that lets all these chips talk to one another – are stuck in the past. Copper simply can’t handle it anymore. We see signal degradation over distance, enormous heat generation, sky-high energy consumption, and physical scaling problems that get worse with every new generation of AI. At the same time, the copper price itself is surging because of AI demand and global electrification. Building data centers the old way isn’t just becoming technically challenging anymore – it’s becoming economically absurd.

So what’s the alternative?

Honestly, there aren’t many good ones left.

We have to move to light.

Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) – where the optics and lasers are placed right next to the chips – changes the game completely. Dramatically lower power consumption, vastly higher bandwidth, and scalability that copper can never match. The biggest hyperscalers and chip companies are already pouring billions into this technology. This isn’t a nice-to-have. It’s a survival requirement for the next generation of AI. Without this shift we will simply hit a hard wall in how far AI can advance.

Just like fiber laid the foundation for today’s digital world, CPO and photonics in general will become the invisible backbone of tomorrow’s AI infrastructure. There is no realistic way around it.

And sitting right in the middle of this perfect storm is Sivers Semiconductors / $SIVEF.

At the very heart of the CPO revolution you need reliable, high-performance laser light sources that can feed these new optical engines efficiently. That is exactly Sivers’ strongest area with their advanced DFB laser arrays. They are already partnering closely with the companies that are building the actual next-generation infrastructure: $GFS and their SCALE CPO platform (targeting a $25 billion pluggable optics market by 2030), $POET, O-Net, $ENA.NE and several others for scalable External Laser Source solutions. 

They combine this with an asset-light model that gives them speed and flexibility to scale without massive capital expenditure, a clear technical edge in customized lasers, proven working demos together with players like Ayar Labs, and natural exposure to AI data centers, SATCOM and defense – markets that all need precisely this kind of photonics.

The broader shift from copper to light in AI infrastructure is heading toward tens of billions of dollars in the coming years. Sivers is positioned where the critical technology meets exploding demand. 

During CMD 2025, CEO Vickram Vathulya highlighted from customer conversations that Sivers seems to be the only ones capable of providing laser arrays in the exact formats and power levels that customers need for these new architectures. A few others do laser arrays, but at much lower power levels.

CTO Andrew McKee added even more color at the same event: 2027 is the target for volume production, with qualification starting this year and pilot ramp-up in 2026. Looking at 2028 forecasts of 19 million AI GPU shipments, and typical architectures needing 5–16 laser sources per GPU, we’re talking hundreds of millions of laser sources per year. An extremely large and exciting addressable market as this rolls out in the data centers.

This isn’t just another incremental upgrade. It’s a fundamental infrastructure transformation.

Back then, many smart people looked at the situation and said “this is good enough – we don’t need to change everything.” History proved them wrong in a spectacular way. Fiber didn’t just improve the internet – it completely unlocked streaming, cloud computing, social media, and the entire digital society we take for granted today. Now the same story is repeating itself, only this time the stakes are dramatically higher. Copper has reached its physical and economic limit. AI is growing at a pace we’ve never seen. Massive clusters with tens of thousands – soon hundreds of thousands – of GPUs that all need to communicate with each other at extreme speeds, constantly. Moore’s Law has kept delivering smaller, faster and more power-efficient transistors on the chips themselves, but the interconnects – the actual nervous system that lets all these chips talk to one another – are stuck in the past. Copper simply can’t handle it anymore. We see signal degradation over distance, enormous heat generation, sky-high energy consumption, and physical scaling problems that get worse with every new generation of AI. At the same time, the copper price itself is surging because of AI demand and global electrification. Building data centers the old way isn’t just becoming technically challenging anymore – it’s becoming economically absurd. So what’s the alternative? Honestly, there aren’t many good ones left. We have to move to light. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) – where the optics and lasers are placed right next to the chips – changes the game completely. Dramatically lower power consumption, vastly higher bandwidth, and scalability that copper can never match. The biggest hyperscalers and chip companies are already pouring billions into this technology. This isn’t a nice-to-have. It’s a survival requirement for the next generation of AI. Without this shift we will simply hit a hard wall in how far AI can advance. Just like fiber laid the foundation for today’s digital world, CPO and photonics in general will become the invisible backbone of tomorrow’s AI infrastructure. There is no realistic way around it. And sitting right in the middle of this perfect storm is Sivers Semiconductors / $SIVEF. At the very heart of the CPO revolution you need reliable, high-performance laser light sources that can feed these new optical engines efficiently. That is exactly Sivers’ strongest area with their advanced DFB laser arrays. They are already partnering closely with the companies that are building the actual next-generation infrastructure: $GFS and their SCALE CPO platform (targeting a $25 billion pluggable optics market by 2030), $POET, O-Net, $ENA.NE and several others for scalable External Laser Source solutions. They combine this with an asset-light model that gives them speed and flexibility to scale without massive capital expenditure, a clear technical edge in customized lasers, proven working demos together with players like Ayar Labs, and natural exposure to AI data centers, SATCOM and defense – markets that all need precisely this kind of photonics. The broader shift from copper to light in AI infrastructure is heading toward tens of billions of dollars in the coming years. Sivers is positioned where the critical technology meets exploding demand. During CMD 2025, CEO Vickram Vathulya highlighted from customer conversations that Sivers seems to be the only ones capable of providing laser arrays in the exact formats and power levels that customers need for these new architectures. A few others do laser arrays, but at much lower power levels. CTO Andrew McKee added even more color at the same event: 2027 is the target for volume production, with qualification starting this year and pilot ramp-up in 2026. Looking at 2028 forecasts of 19 million AI GPU shipments, and typical architectures needing 5–16 laser sources per GPU, we’re talking hundreds of millions of laser sources per year. An extremely large and exciting addressable market as this rolls out in the data centers. This isn’t just another incremental upgrade. It’s a fundamental infrastructure transformation. The ones who understand this early will see why photonics is becoming one of the clearest and strongest tailwinds in the entire AI value chain. $SIVE feels extremely well positioned."}}}, "rest_id": "2065889424605618466", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11369", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

#3 on the schwerelos board YTD, 62.9% win rate across 62 scored calls.

Photonics is in the early phase 2, which is almost pre-acceleration. Then comes the mature phase. That’s where memory and chips are. 

There's a long way to go. I would add as many shares as I could in $SIVE, $AAOI, and the others. Bullish signs. 

Current photonic stocks in my portfolio: $SIVE, $AAOI, $CRDO (swing).

What’s your favorite or biggest bet?

Most people still don’t see it. Photonics is where Memory was a year ago. That’s why we’re still VERY early. The pattern is clear if you’ve been watching this AI super cycle unfold since 2022. > Chips kicked everything off after ChatGPT dropped in late 2022/early 2023. $NVDA went nuclear with Hopper H100s, and then Blackwell came. $AMD started carving out real share in GPUs and custom ASICs. > Memory followed with a lag. Mid-2024 onward, HBM went into massive shortage as AI training and inference sucked up capacity. That pulled DRAM and NAND higher too. Micron $MU, SK Hynix, Samsung and other memory players delivered strong re-ratings as pricing power returned. > Now in mid-2026, memory is deep in its prolonged supercycle - sold out for years ahead (confirmed by Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra). Chips are in a more mature phase, still growing strong with 1:1 CPU to GPU ratios as AI agents scale, and will sustain into the second half of this decade as Lisa Su says compute capacity will 100x by 2030. But what’s next? Photonics, folks! - AI clusters are suffering from constraints related to power, heat, latency and bandwidth. Optics solves this. That is why the next MAJOR leg is clearly SiPh and CPO. - 1.6T pluggables are ramping, early CPO deployments are starting but nowhere near to the scale they will reach because real mass adoption and deep in-package integration will come in 2027-2028+. - Goldman Sach predicts TAM will explode 10x by the end of this decade. To better understand this, if one divides it into 3 common phases across the supercycles (Early, Acceleration, Maturity), we see that: - Chips are clearly in Phase 3 - Memory is in Phase 2/3 - Photonics is very early in Phase 2 🔥 The AI buildout keeps shifting to the next bottleneck because the world needs more compute than ever before. text:
Everyone’s watching $SNAP tomorrow for the hardware reveal. 💦

Now the keynote itself. 30 minutes.

9:30am-10:00am PT tomorrow. Main Stage.

Titled “Making Computing More Human.”

30 minutes????

That’s a short slot for a CEO. Which means either:

30 minutes is not a demo.

You can’t demo spatial computing in 30 minutes.

You can’t show developers new tools in 30 minutes.

You can’t unveil a hardware platform in 30 minutes.

30 minutes is an announcement. 📢 

Evan loves a Jobs playbook. 

Jobs announced iPhone in 90 minutes.

Spiegel has a third of that.

Which means the 30 minutes isn’t the reveal.

✨It’s the headline.✨

The partner who walks on stage unannounced is the story…

So 

A) It’s a teaser - product shown, details later

B) The 30 minutes is the most densely packed announcement in Snap’s history… doubt it. 

C) The real announcement happens after the keynote. A special session, a press release, a partner who walks on stage unannounced

The “unannounced guest” scenario is the most Jobs-like move possible.

Jobs never pre-announced guest appearances. AT&T’s CEO appeared on stage at iPhone launch unannounced. The carrier partnership was the surprise.

Now run the strategic maths:

Microsoft bet $13B on OpenAI.
Google bet $3B on Anthropic.
Amazon bet $4B on Anthropic.
NVIDIA bet $5B on Intel and secured valuable IP, capacity, technology as a hedge. 

Every trillion dollar company is buying a stake in the next PLATFORM. 

Apple has $132B in cash.

A $2B stake in Specs Inc is 1.5% of their war chest.

To get:
1. AR glasses 12-24 months ahead of the market.
2. True AR display vertically integrated stack
with WaveOptics and Compound Photonics owned IP
3. 8 years of spatial mapping they can’t replicate (Illumix spatial stack)
4. 956M MAU - the social layer they never built
5. Cultural relevance in the genz demo. 
6. 400k developers already building the next OS.

The man who sold the last thing Apple bought just joined the board.

So,

30 minutes on stage tomorrow…

👻🍏📢

$SNAP #AWE #specs
