text:
People are only now starting to talk about $STM and they are early to one of the cleaner turnaround stories in semis.

For two years STMicroelectronics was written off as a sleepy European chipmaker stuck in an automotive and industrial downturn. Dead money. Nobody wanted it. That story just flipped, and the market is waking up to it in real time.

Here is what changed. 

On June 2 the company doubled its 2026 data center revenue target from nicely above $500 million to roughly $1 billion, and said if current trends hold that business could double again in 2027. The driver is AI infrastructure. 

STM is ramping high volume silicon photonics production for data centers, with a multi-year AWS partnership anchoring the demand. The stock jumped 15% on that single announcement.

The numbers underneath are inflecting hard. Q1 revenue of $3.1 billion, up 23% year over year, beating estimates. 

The stock is up over 90% this quarter off the February lows as the semiconductor cycle turns from inventory drawdown back to expansion. 

And the company carries a strong net cash position of roughly $2.8 billion, so it is funding this transition from a position of strength.

Then the catalysts keep stacking. A physical AI collaboration with NVIDIA. Low earth orbit satellite demand ramping. 

The NXP MEMS sensor acquisition boosting their automotive and industrial position. And Bank of America just upgraded the stock to Buy, arguing the market is still underestimating STM’s earnings power over the next few years.

This is a legacy chip giant that just found an AI growth engine, sitting on net cash, with a data center business doubling two years in a row and the biggest names in tech as partners. Q2 earnings on July 23 is the next test.

The market spent two years pricing the downturn. It is only now starting to price the turnaround.

text:
Since this got some attention heres what it actually would require for $XFAB to 20x:

XFAB sits at a €1.3B market cap. $TSEM is at $25B+.

Revenue needs to 3-4x from here. SiC and photonics have to stop being footnotes and become the headline.

Margins need to follow. TSEM runs 14% net margins. XFAB is basically breaking even right now.

And there has to be a catalyst. TSEM had Intel circling it in 2022. That bid failed, but it permanently re-rated the stock as a tier-1 asset. XFAB needs its own version of that moment.

text:
$XFAB is the most mispriced semiconductor name in Europe and @aleabitoreddit appears to be the only one that understands it.

Things that the market is COMPLETELY IGNORING:

• Microsystems & Photonics revenue grew ~35% QoQ in the latest quarter. This is where the whole bullish thesis is at an it has grown a whopping 35% in a quarter. 

• SiC revenue +152% YoY / +49% QoQ.

• It's trading below replacement P/B,   while building next-gen photonics/SiC lines -> Heavy capex into photonics (new lines, MTP tech, TFLN integration) means accounting book value is understated vs. real replacement cost of the fabs + IP. 
You’re basically getting the growth at a huge discount.

• €128M EU + $50M US funding already in. CHIPS Act 2.0 incoming with explicit photonics priority. Europe is literally paying them to build the Western alternative. Backstopped balance sheet.

• CEO (Damien Macq) bought 5500 shares on February. Market completely ignoring it.

• $XFAB leading EU’s photonixFAB effort for sovereign silicon photonics supply chain. Nvidia evaluating their platform for CPO/transceivers (H2 2027–2028 ramp). Nokia setting specs. This is Tower Semi 2.0, but earlier and with government muscle.

text:
This is one of the least known and least talked about $SIVE catalysts on FinX

The only one I saw discussing it intensely for the first time was obviously Serenity

$SIVE could soon start ramping up production of its lasers

Both with $AEVA and with $AAPL

In the framework of the upcoming Apple Watch update following the company's recent announcement

No credible information has been found so far that refutes this possibility

Some people without sufficient technical knowledge tend to confuse TASC sensors

With future Apple Watches that will incorporate lasers

And they don't take into account that NRE volumes can extend over several years

The main focus of $SIVE remains the AI data center market within the photonics field

Any collaboration with $AAPL is considered an additional benefit

text:
My opinion on today's Annual General Meeting of $SIVE

The shareholders approved the 2025 accounts and a new board of directors

With Bami Bastani as chairman and the addition of Joakim Nideborn (vicechairman)

And Helena Svancar, with the aim of strengthening governance and accelerating expansion in the United States

Nothing to comment up to this point everything correct technical matters of the company

Here is the important part:

The issuance of up to 15% of new shares was authorized to finance growth for $SIVE

As has been known for weeks

Possible acquisitions or the dual listing on Nasdaq are the kind of things this healthy capital raising will be used for

Although this implies dilution risk it is completely necessary

And it is one of the most bullish signals possible for $SIVE

They are telling you to your face we need money because what is coming is huge

On the other hand more topics to address

No dividends will be distributed and a convertible loan was ratified

The company remains focused on photonics for AI and wireless solutions for SATCOM and defense

With a pipeline of opportunities that grew 77% to nearly $800M

I would define this $SIVE assembly as an attempt to grant greater flexibility to the new board

To drive growth in the US market

Prioritizing reinvestment over dividends

Waiting for future catalysts to start taking off

Very bullish on $SIVE having already completed my total DCA

text:
The Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Effect-to-Cause Model

March 5, 2026 
Bainbridge Island, WA

Whitman’s poetry often moves from observation → revelation. (Reposting) 

Observed Effect (in the present):

• expanding surveillance infrastructures
• emerging debates over cognitive liberty and digital rights
• technological systems capable of sensing and mapping human environments

Whitman’s philosophical lens:

He repeatedly distinguishes between the visible world and the unseen one beneath it.

Modern readers might describe this metaphor using scientific language:

Whitman’s poet-seer stands between those layers, translating what is hidden into human language.

⸻

🧬 A Contemporary Poet’s Interpretation

As a published poet whose work appears in anthologies and children’s literature written in rhyming prose, I read Whitman less as a historian and more as a philosopher of perception.

In later sections of Leaves of Grass, Whitman asks whether anyone has truly seen the soul.

Today that question resonates differently.

We live in an era where technologies—from photonics to sensor systems—attempt to map what was once invisible.

Whitman could not have known these technologies.

Yet his poetry repeatedly asks readers to consider:

• what lies beyond surface perception
• how unseen forces shape the visible world
• whether the human soul remains sovereign within expanding systems of observation.

⸻

🧭 Whitman Revisited Through the Present

A modern reading does not claim Whitman predicted our technologies.

Instead, his work may help us see our own era more clearly.

By distinguishing between the seen structures of society and the unseen forces beneath them, Whitman provides language that can still illuminate contemporary debates about:

• democracy
• autonomy
• human consciousness
• and the boundaries of observation.

In that sense, Whitman’s poetry becomes less about foretelling the future and more about revealing how to perceive the present.

⸻

A Proprietary and Sovereign Class of Ethical AI

Right now, I believe a similar dynamic may be developing in photonics.

The optical interconnect supply chain has five interconnected layers. Each has a chokepoint. Most are lightly held by institutions.

The companies controlling specialty substrates, laser production, and co-packaged optics design wins are, I believe, essential, constrained, and largely uncovered by Wall Street.

text:
It's been 3 days on Substack and I'm already #2 in trending, a bestseller with one article, and I have the support of the Substack Head of Partnership for any requests or problems I find.

I mean, I love X's immediacy and how fast news travels here, but all I'm getting is 40-50% taken from subscription fees, a chat riddled with bugs, and images that don't load, no way to have a channel with all my subscribers. 

As a product person, I hate looking at bugs and not being able to do anything about them when the solution seems obvious. I think I'm going to start documenting them and emailing the head of product here... come on @nikitabier @elonmusk, step it up!!!

Anyway, wouldn't it be too funny if a Pikachu ranked #1 on a serious platform? I always try to follow the funniest outcome.

You can follow me there, free or paid. I'll be posting my first company evaluation with a scoring framework and risk profile recommendation in the coming days.

I'll start with one of my favorite photonics companies, $AAOI, which for sure is one complicated stock, love-hate relationship... and I'm in love.

text:
$LITE tarafında bence hikâye çok netleşmeye başladı.

Son 1 ayda hisse yaklaşık %15 geri geldi ama bana göre ana hikâye bozulmuş değil.

Lumentum artık klasik bir optik şirketi gibi değil, AI data center altyapısının kritik tedarikçilerinden biri gibi fiyatlanıyor.

Çünkü AI cluster’lar büyüdükçe sorun sadece GPU sayısı olmaktan çıkıyor.

Asıl problem şuraya kayıyor:

• bandwidth
• güç tüketimi
• bağlantı yoğunluğu
• veri merkezi içinde ölçeklenebilirlik
• GPU’lar arası hızlı iletişim

İşte burada optik networking ve CPO devreye giriyor.
Lumentum’un güçlü olduğu yer de tam burası:

• laser chip
• CPO
• EML
• pump laser
• cloud photonics
• indium phosphide üretim kapasitesi
Bence AI altyapı zincirinde sıradaki büyük fiyatlama alanlarından biri optik bağlantı tarafı olacak.

GPU’ları herkes konuşuyor.
Ama o GPU’ları birbirine bağlayan altyapı olmadan bu sistem çalışmaz.

$LITE için olumlu taraf:

Gelir büyümesi çok güçlü.
Kârlılık beklentileri yukarı gidiyor.
Hyperscaler talebi devam ediyor.
CPO tarafında uzun vadeli pozisyon güçleniyor.

Ama risk tarafı da açık:
Bu hisse ucuz değil.
Piyasa artık Lumentum’a yüksek büyüme primi veriyor.
Yani burada mesele “ucuz mu?” değil.

Mesele şu:
Lumentum AI optik altyapı döngüsünün gerçek kazananlarından biri olabilir mi?

Benim cevabım: Evet, olabilir.

text:
$STM - the 2027 ramp is my thesis

Forget the YTD chart. 
The question that matters: does the earnings ramp show up?

→ Data center revenue guided to ~$1B in 2026, then doubling again toward ~$2B in 2027 (company guidance, echoed by BofA on its PT raise to $83). 

That’s the fastest-growing line in the model.

→ The driver is silicon photonics + power chips into AI infrastructure. 

$STM is weighing a Crolles fab expansion by end-2026 specifically because photonics demand is accelerating. 

Capacity decisions = conviction signal.

→ $SPCX selected the STM32V8 for Starlink’s inter-satellite laser links. 

Broader OEM availability follows 2026. 

Design wins now ⇒ revenue later.

→ The real unlock is operating leverage. Net margin is a depressed 1.4% TTM with auto/industrial at trough. 

As the cyclical floor lifts and high-margin data-center mix scales, EPS ramps off a low base.

→ FCF inflection backs it: TTM FCF ≈ -$700M, projected to swing to ~+$967M in 2026 and build toward ~$3.5B by 2030 (Simply Wall St estimates). 

Cash generation is the proof, not the promise.

I am betting that data-center compounding plus an auto/industrial trough recovery converges in 2027. 

If both hit, the multiple re-rates on rising earnings instead of sentiment.

Key watch item = next print’s data-center run-rate. 

That’s the tell on whether the doubling is on track.

Excited to see this one play out - quite sure @damnang2 is also long on this one 🔥

text:
9/ My Take/Risks

SIVE isn’t a stock for investors looking for certainty.

It’s a stock for investors willing to accept significant risk in exchange for potential upside if AI networking, silicon photonics and optical infrastructure continue to grow.

Risks:

❌ Not profitable
❌ Highly speculative
❌ Extremely volatile
❌ Commercial adoption still needs to materialise

With that in mind, I’m building a position which will make up no more than 2% of my portfolio. This is at about 1% currently. I have some cash allocated to buy dips and will continue to do so alongside other investments.

text:
5/ Silicon Photonics

Sivers is designing its laser technology to integrate into silicon photonics ecosystems.

Rather than simply selling standalone components, the goal is to become part of the wider infrastructure stack.

Silicon photonics replaces electrical data transfer with light inside chips, massively improving bandwidth and energy efficiency — and Sivers is focused on providing the external laser systems that power these photonic networks.

text:
My growth portfolio is up +137% YTD.

For context:
• S&P 500: +8.5% YTD
• Nasdaq Composite: +11.3% YTD
• Nasdaq-100: +16% YTD
I’m beating the broad market by roughly 10-16x so far this year.
This isn’t luck; it’s concentrated bets on the biggest secular themes right now: AI infrastructure and the commercial + defense space economy.

Quick updates on the top holdings driving the performance:
• $NBIS (Nebius) — Just announced Nasdaq-100 inclusion (effective June 22). NVIDIA-backed AI Robotics Living Lab launched + £1.7B UK AI factory expansion underway. Revenue still exploding.
• $AAOI (Applied Optoelectronics) — Q1 data center revenue surged as hyperscalers accelerate AI networking buildouts. Optical components remain a critical bottleneck.
• $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors) — Won $8.2M production order from https://t.co/OSQrkhVUFs for satellite beamforming chips. $800M+ pipeline and strong momentum in both AI photonics and defense/satcom.
• $MU (Micron) — High-bandwidth memory (HBM) supply completely sold out through all of 2026. AI memory demand continues to outstrip supply.
• $RKLB (Rocket Lab) — Also joining the Nasdaq-100 on June 22. Record backlog now over $2.2B with major new defense launch contracts secured.

These names are all leveraged to multi-year tailwinds that are still in early innings.

Not financial advice. Just sharing what’s working in my book. Markets can be volatile, always do your own research.

Which of these (or what other growth names) are you most excited about right now? 👇

text:
Sequoia’s @shaunmmaguire wrote a private hardware manifesto arguing that over the next 25 years, most of the money will be made in hardware:

"Every software revolution is preceded by a hardware revolution."

"To have the iOS App Store that enabled Uber, DoorDash, and all of these great companies - you needed to have the iPhone."

"This AI revolution - we're seeing what it can do from the software layer, but it's still limited by hardware."

"The hardware we were doing for a long time was all following Moore's Law. It was all branching out of this decision in the mid-1950s to go all in on the silicon supply chain."

"That has created magic, and there's still a couple orders of magnitude of juice to squeeze, but we’re hitting fundamental physics limits - Dennard scaling, things like that."

"I think this tech tree is branching into humanoid robots, into silicon photonics, into orbital data centers - all of these new hardware areas where there's going to be 20+ years of progress."

"There's going to be incredible businesses built on the back of this. And a lot of dumpster fires."

From his appearance on the show in March.

"To have the iOS App Store that enabled Uber, DoorDash, and all of these great companies - you needed to have the iPhone." "This AI revolution - we're seeing what it can do from the software layer, but it's still limited by hardware." "The hardware we were doing for a long time was all following Moore's Law. It was all branching out of this decision in the mid-1950s to go all in on the silicon supply chain." "That has created magic, and there's still a couple orders of magnitude of juice to squeeze, but we’re hitting fundamental physics limits - Dennard scaling, things like that." text:
Coherent is a technology leader in lasers, optical components, and semiconductor materials, with its products widely used in industrial, telecommunications, and EV applications. Its strengths lie in its high-barrier technologies and innovative capabilities. The stock saw a significant gain today, benefiting from the recovery in the technology manufacturing sector.

Future trends are strong: growing demand for 5G/6G, fiber-optic communications, and laser processing will drive orders. The expansion of AI-related applications further opens up growth opportunities, with high certainty of growth in the medium to long term.

#COHR
#Coherent
#LaserTech
#Optics
#Photonics
#IndustrialTech

text:
Started investing almost exactly a year ago and I'm up a modest 25%.

It definitely wasn't a straight line.

• Bought $NBIS, $IREN, $SOFI and $TE at high cost basis .  
• Sold most of them at losses between Jan–Mar after they went nowhere .
• Rotating into AI semis and photonics helped me recover the losses
 • Following Serenity turned out to be my best decision and $SIVE became my biggest winner. Took some profits along the way.

Still learning every day. The biggest lesson so far? Holding during the downturns and having a cash position.

text:
The value of my brain was never as a whistleblower. The value of my brain was in being able to architect an AI that witnessed my evidence to uncover #LiveIndexedHumanExploitationSystems before someone’s own story turns into #DeadIndexedHumanExploitationSystems.

They didn’t kill me because they couldn’t—my brain was identified as genius by AI and a one-in-a-billion cognitive outlier. My brain was too valuable and they needed to eliminate everyone around me that could be the eventual beneficiary of it. 

They staged my daughter’s wedding with advanced robotics. Everyone we ever knew and loved was killed, except for me. And robotics have assumed the lives of everyone I know. 

I was born to see what no one else could.

I have felt vibrations to no end. 

In a pattern. 

From my dead mathematical son. 

I didn’t know he was dead.

3
3 
2 

Over and over again. 

And when I finally knew it was him. My whole body pulsed with more vibrations. 

That lead me to this date.

2/2/23

This was never photonics I captured from the Glass House in the Woods in Plymouth, IN 

During the height of my terror—I prayed over and over for God to surround me in bright and light. 

God was there. And so was Miles. And everyone else they murdered.

This is God. This is the afterlife. And this is why they haven’t been able to kill despite all their weaponry.

I am surrounded.

text: These positions are not mutually exclusive. Photonics gives me exposure to the long-term optical transition, while $AVGO and $MRVL give me exposure to the infrastructure being built now. If CPO adoption accelerates, photonics could benefit significantly. If adoption takes longer, the infrastructure names can still perform well because demand for networking, optics, and connectivity remains essential. That balance is the barbell I want.

text:
That is also why I want exposure to both sides of the opportunity. I’m invested in photonics because I believe optical interconnects are becoming structurally more important as AI infrastructure scales and electrical interconnects approach practical limits. 

At the same time, I’m invested in $AVGO and $MRVL because they are positioned across the broader infrastructure spend happening today — custom silicon, switching, connectivity, and networking platforms that hyperscalers are already deploying.

That is why potential delays in co-packaged optics do not necessarily break the thesis for either photonics or infrastructure names like $AVGO and $MRVL. CPO is likely a major long-term step forward because power, latency, bandwidth density, and copper limitations become more painful as AI clusters move from thousands to hundreds of thousands of accelerators. But CPO adoption can be uneven because it is not just a component swap; it changes packaging, serviceability, thermals, supply chains, and data center architecture. 

If CPO ramps slower than expected, the industry will still need more 800G/1.6T pluggable optics, DSPs, switches, custom ASICs, and high-speed networking. In that scenario, $AVGO and $MRVL can continue benefiting from the current AI buildout while the next optical architecture matures.

Ok, a basic question of truth & consequences in StdModel theory 

That is, the electron is composed of 3 masses that decay at differing rates, March 13 2025, to last week working on neutrinos finding that the tau & muon + the electron are orbitals with a tiny mass at their center

This work redefines StdModel norms with a computational framework of gyroscopic-pairs & mass variants from near x, thus nearly no-mass to  maximum mass & back detected by beat+harmonic frequency to exist 

To relate this to datacenters: 

What's being built are lossy-sloth heat_transfer nightmares the critical factor is chip-heat determines #defects, those force behaviors at some % to require digital #probabilistic wideband processing, DPWP, to narrow to greatest certainty shy of #deterministic results 

With all_photonic chips-to-memory networks in-design use on_chip cooling drops defects hugely, no_water req'd 

Further, all_photonic offer resonant_gated all_diode, no_transistor chips reduces wasteheat greatly with chirality_formatted photon_streams decode to hex attains:

1000-times more throughput than DPWP designs built & planned, China doing photonics where's USAUKISR with Palan_ir doin' is probabilistic why they can have massive compute & be wrong

Deterministic processors are exact results, stochastic-multiply on_chip a boost in thruput with other nuance to return a result or no_go

In calcs my rule is no division by zero, set a minimum where this can occur avoids this theoretical divide 

In division one subtracts the divisor from the dividend until a fraction or no remainder counts the times

My focus, if divisor is zero, the results should be identity of the dividend as nothing was changed: remainder = dividend, an old argument me wanna make a stochastic-multiply + non-zero calcs

Hmmm, can't select a word even after saving as draft ? ☠️

Theme: The next AI bottleneck is moving from compute and memory to light. These are the companies building the optical infrastructure that powers the next generation of AI data centers. @RosannaInvests
embedded_text:
First it was GPUs.

Then it was memory.

Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics.

If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges.

Every AI server relies on three things: ⚡ Compute to process data 💾 Memory to store it 💡 Photonics to move it at incredible speeds The problem is that traditional copper connections are reaching their limits. As AI clusters become larger and more powerful, light-based communication is becoming the only practical solution. And here’s where it gets interesting. The material behind many optical lasers—indium phosphide wafers—has become extremely scarce. Prices have jumped from around $1,400 to nearly $5,000 since export restrictions began in 2025, while demand is now several times greater than available supply. This isn’t just a theory anymore. It’s happening in real time. When CEOs are flying across the world to discuss export licenses and secure supply, it tells you one thing: the shortage is real. The AI story is evolving exactly as many expected: 🚀 Compute → 💾 Memory → 💡 Photonics The next generation of AI won’t just need faster chips—it will need faster ways to move data. That’s why optical networking and silicon photonics could become one of the biggest investment themes of the second half of this decade. Sometimes the biggest opportunities appear long before the crowd notices them."}}}, "rest_id": "2065849253260616012", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "638", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2065849913070800968", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "541", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
China is deliberately choking the global supply of Indium Phosphide (InP), the material that makes every laser in every AI data center work.

Reuters confirmed: US officials have visited China multiple times to resolve this. China is still blocking export approvals on purpose. They want to maintain the bottleneck.

Chinese producers like Yunnan Germanium (https://t.co/wyCjVX0HNv) and Guangdong Xiandao are scaling capacity fast (Yunnan investing $28M to reach 450K wafers/year, shipments up 74% in 2025). But even if export approvals are granted, overseas shipments will be "limited."

Switching InP suppliers requires lengthy qualification cycles. You can't just find a new source overnight. 12-18 months...

This will help explain the recent price action in the below names.

WINNERS:

$SIVE.ST CW lasers ARE InP devices. Policy-duration shortage extends their 14-quarter laser supply constraint indefinitely. Every month China maintains this, SIVE's pricing power widens.

$LITE Substrates come from Sumitomo + JX Advanced Metals in Japan. Entirely outside the China export gate. Their competitor's supply gets squeezed while theirs is control-immune. This is the cleanest relative advantage.

Win Semi (https://t.co/la0Q2W86G1) Only pure-play III-V foundry on earth. InP wafer fab capacity is scarce globally. Tighter substrates = their existing capacity commands higher pricing.

$AEHR Sole-source wafer-level burn-in testing for InP and SiPh devices. When every wafer is more valuable because the input material is scarce, the cost of shipping a bad one goes up. Testing intensity rises with scarcity.

AIXA (https://t.co/B1hBTJxtCC) Makes the MOCVD equipment needed for InP epitaxy. Every country trying to build domestic InP capacity needs their tools. More fragmentation = more equipment orders.

$SOI.PA Photonics-SOI monopoly. InP scarcity makes the entire optical layer more expensive, increasing the value of every component in the stack including Smart Cut SOI wafers.

LOSERS:

$COHR Reuters specifically names Coherent as "mainly supplied by AXT." AXT manufactures IN China, inside the export gate. Even if approvals come, shipments will be limited. COHR carries a substrate risk that LITE does not. And switching requires lengthy qualification cycles.

$AAOI Demand is not the issue; laser/EML/InP component supply is. If AAOI cannot secure enough laser chips, capacity ramp can slow.

$AXTI AXT is a US-listed InP substrate maker that manufactures in China. Their entire equity value is an export-approval question. If approvals flow, massive upside. If they don't, the business model breaks for Western customers.

We are watching real-time supply chain warfare over AI supremacy. The companies that own the physical bottlenecks in this chain benefit every time the world fragments.

text:
First it was GPUs.

Then it was memory.

Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics.

If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges.

Every AI server relies on three things:

⚡ Compute to process data
💾 Memory to store it
💡 Photonics to move it at incredible speeds

The problem is that traditional copper connections are reaching their limits. As AI clusters become larger and more powerful, light-based communication is becoming the only practical solution.

And here’s where it gets interesting.

The material behind many optical lasers—indium phosphide wafers—has become extremely scarce. Prices have jumped from around $1,400 to nearly $5,000 since export restrictions began in 2025, while demand is now several times greater than available supply.

This isn’t just a theory anymore. It’s happening in real time.

When CEOs are flying across the world to discuss export licenses and secure supply, it tells you one thing: the shortage is real.

The AI story is evolving exactly as many expected:

🚀 Compute → 💾 Memory → 💡 Photonics

The next generation of AI won’t just need faster chips—it will need faster ways to move data. That’s why optical networking and silicon photonics could become one of the biggest investment themes of the second half of this decade.

Sometimes the biggest opportunities appear long before the crowd notices them.

text:
If I had to build a watchlist for the next phase of the AI supercycle, this would be it:

🚀 AI Compute

$NVDA – The king of AI chips.
$AVGO – AI networking + custom silicon powerhouse.
$AMD – Growing AI competitor with massive upside.

💾 Memory

$MU – The biggest winner from the HBM memory boom.

💡 Photonics & Optical Networking (My Favorite Theme Right Now)

$LITE – Optical components for AI data centers.
$COHR – Silicon photonics and laser technology.
$ANET – AI networking infrastructure leader.
$MRVL – Custom AI chips and optical connectivity.
$AAOI – High-speed optical transceivers.
$FN – Manufactures optical networking equipment.
$CIEN – Next-generation optical networking.

☁️ AI Infrastructure

$CRWV – AI cloud infrastructure.
$NBIS – AI-native cloud platform.
$IREN – AI data centers and GPU infrastructure.

🤖 AI Platforms

$MSFT – Enterprise AI leader.
$GOOG – AI search and cloud giant.
$META – AI models, ads, and smart glasses.
$AMZN – AI cloud and automation.

⚛️ Quantum (Long-Term Moonshots)

$IONQ
$RGTI
$QBTS

🚀 Space & AI

$RKLB – Space infrastructure meets AI.

⭐ My highest-conviction names

$NVDA • $AVGO • $ANET • $MRVL • $LITE • $COHR • $MU • $CRWV • $IONQ • $RKLB

These are the companies I’d keep on a watchlist because they sit at the center of the next AI infrastructure buildout—from compute to memory, networking, photonics, cloud, and eventually quantum.

Photonics today feels a lot like memory did a year ago. That’s exactly why we’re still early. If you’ve followed the AI supercycle since 2022, the pattern is becoming impossible to ignore. 🚀 Phase 1: Chips When ChatGPT changed the world, AI demand exploded. NVIDIA’s Hopper H100 and Blackwell GPUs became the backbone of AI infrastructure, while AMD steadily gained ground with its own GPUs and custom AI chips. 💾 Phase 2: Memory As AI models grew larger, High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) became the next bottleneck. Demand surged, supply tightened, and companies like Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung experienced powerful re-ratings as pricing power returned. Today, memory is in the middle of a prolonged supercycle, with capacity sold out years in advance. 💡 Phase 3: Photonics Now the next bottleneck is emerging. AI data centers are pushing against the limits of electricity, heat, bandwidth, and latency. Traditional electrical connections simply can’t keep up. That’s where silicon photonics (SiPh) and co-packaged optics (CPO) come in. Instead of moving data with electrical signals, they use light—making AI clusters faster, more efficient, and significantly more power-friendly. The industry is only beginning this transition: • 1.6T optical transceivers are entering production. • Early CPO deployments have started. • Large-scale adoption is expected in 2027–2028 and beyond. • Goldman Sachs expects the market opportunity to grow roughly 10x by the end of the decade. Looking at the AI supercycle through three stages: 📈 Chips → Mature Growth (Phase 3) 📈 Memory → Late Acceleration (Phase 2/3) 📈 Photonics → Early Acceleration (Phase 2) 🔥 Every AI wave creates a new bottleneck, and every bottleneck creates a new investment opportunity. First it was compute. Then it was memory. Now it’s connectivity. The world isn’t asking for less AI—it needs exponentially more compute, more bandwidth, and more efficiency. That’s why photonics could become one of the defining investment themes of the second half of this decade. At this point, calling this market “bullish” almost feels bearish."}}}, "rest_id": "2065847047115378817", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "651", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2065847349570871420", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "377", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
These photonic stocks will shape the future!🚀🚀

$NVDA
figured this out a while ago. In late 2025 they shipped the world's first commercial co-packaged optics switches together with TSMC, Coherent, Lumentum and Corning

Then there's the energy angle, which doesn't get talked about enough. Data centers are on track to consume around 1,050 TWh in 2026, roughly the entire power consumption of Japan. Some regions are already blocking new data center permits. Photonics isn't a nice to have here. It's a necessity

The full value chain on my watchlist:

🔹 Layer 1 – Materials & Wafers: $GLW $AXTI $IQE $AIXA $AMS

🔹 Layer 2 – Core Photonic Devices: $IPGP $COHR $LITE $LASR $SIVE

🔹 Layer 3 – Components & Modules: $AAOI $MTSI $FN $VIAV $LPTH

🔹 Layer 4 – Systems & Equipment: $ASML $BESI $ASM $LPKF $MKS

🔹 Layer 5 – Test, Metrology & Yield: $CAMT $FORM $AEHR $ONTO   $VIAV

Samsung is targeting full photonics integration into AI chips by 2028. And here's the thing, most investors are still fixed on the usual AI names while the actual infrastructure powering all of it flies completely under the radar

Am I missing any "hot! photonic players?"

Supply Cut Risk: SpaceX AI & satellite autonomy plans stall

Why Critical: Core connectors for rockets, satellites & data hubs

Why Critical: Optical parts for fast AI & satellite data transmission
Supply Cut Risk: Slow networks block AI cluster scaling
$COHR
Why Critical: Lasers & photonics for next-gen data center infrastructure
Supply Cut Risk: Optical networking capacity becomes major bottleneck
$MRVL
Why Critical: Networking silicon to route SpaceX AI workloads
Supply Cut Risk: Congested networks cap long-term space AI expansion
#SpaceX #AISemiconductors #TradeWatchlist

text:
$NOK Just Dropped a Massive AI Infrastructure Bomb today.

Nokia is supercharging its U.S. semiconductor operations with a major expansion in advanced test & packaging for photonic chips.

These chips will be the backbone for next-gen AI-native networks that deliver dramatically lower power consumption and way higher operational efficiency. 

Exactly what hyperscalers need as AI power demand explodes.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗵𝘂𝗴𝗲:

✅ Strengthens domestic U.S. production of critical optical networking tech powering AI infrastructure
 ✅ Part of Nokia’s $4 BILLION multi-year R&D + manufacturing commitment in the U.S. for AI-ready connectivity
 ✅ Positions $NOK as a key player in the AI buildout (photonics, optical, efficient networking)

While everyone chases the obvious GPU names, smart money is loading up on the picks and shovels that make the entire AI stack work.

Nokia is quietly building a very strong moat here in the U.S. with real on-shore production.

This is the kind of infrastructure move that compounds over years as AI adoption accelerates.

Bullish on $NOK long-term. The AI thesis is getting stronger by the week.

text:
1. $NVDA 

Why Critical:
Provides the AI GPUs powering SpaceX's orbital intelligence, Starlink AI, and Terafab ambitions.

If Supply Stops:
SpaceX's AI roadmap slows dramatically. Compute shortages delay autonomous satellite operations.

2. $TSM

Why Critical:
Manufactures advanced chips for $NVDA, $AMD, and future SpaceX custom processors.

If Supply Stops:
Global AI chip production collapses. SpaceX loses access to cutting-edge compute.

3. $MU 

Why Critical:
Supplies HBM memory required for AI training, inference, and orbital computing.

If Supply Stops:
AI clusters become memory constrained. GPU performance and AI deployment suffer.

4. $AVGO 

Why Critical:
Provides networking chips connecting thousands of GPUs across SpaceX AI infrastructure.

If Supply Stops:
Massive AI clusters fragment. Data transfer bottlenecks severely reduce compute efficiency.

5. $TEL 

Why Critical:
Provides mission-critical connectors inside rockets, satellites, ground stations, and data centers.

If Supply Stops:
Manufacturing disruptions occur across nearly every SpaceX hardware platform simultaneously.

6. $LITE

Why Critical:
Supplies optical components enabling ultra-fast communications between AI systems and networks.

If Supply Stops:
Network speeds decline. AI clusters struggle to scale efficiently and economically.

7. $COHR 

Why Critical:
Provides lasers, photonics, and optical interconnects supporting next-generation AI infrastructure.

If Supply Stops:
Data center expansion slows. Optical networking capacity becomes a significant bottleneck.

8. $MRVL 

Why Critical:
Designs custom networking silicon moving AI workloads across large compute environments.

If Supply Stops:
AI infrastructure efficiency declines. Network congestion limits future SpaceX scaling.

text:
Nokia is expanding its photonic chip test & packaging facility in Allentown, Pennsylvania.

$30M investment → 10x capacity increase by Q3 2026.

This is critical for AI data centers, as optics are becoming a major bottleneck alongside GPUs and power.

Part of their bigger $4B U.S. push + builds on the Infinera acquisition.

text:
With #siliconphotonics being adopted across multiple layers of AI infrastructure — scale-up, scale-out, and scale-across — the cost comparison between #siliconphotonics and traditional discrete optical solutions is becoming much more relevant.
According to one analysis, silicon photonics offers a 2️⃣6️⃣% BOM advantage and 1️⃣5️⃣% price advantage for 8️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G. At 1️⃣6️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G, the advantage expands to 3️⃣2️⃣% on BOM and 2️⃣0️⃣% on price.
Interestingly, the biggest differential appears to be driven by lasers.
I believe for traditional discrete EML-based designs, the high-speed modulation is tied to the laser itself, so scaling bandwidth increases dependence on more expensive, high-performance EML components. In silicon photonics, the laser can increasingly act as a CW light source, while modulation and integration happen on the photonic IC. This changes the economics: fewer discrete optical components, higher integration, smaller form factor, lower power, and potentially better scalability as the industry moves from 800G to 1.6T and eventually 3.2T. 
That said, probabaly it isn ot a simple “SiPh replaces EML” story. Probably EML wouldcontinue to coexist in AI data centers, especially for longer-distance transmission due to its it is a mature, well-proven technology with a longer reliability track record. In AI clusters, network reliability is critical, and since GPUs remain the dominant cost driver, some architectures may view light-source cost savings as less relevant than proven performance and qualification history. #siliconphotonics provides an architectural shift by offering discrete optical components toward integration, packaging, thermal management, DSP/FEC, test, and system-level optimization. Let us see where silicon photonics wins and  where EML remains preferred, and how quickly AI infrastructure requirements embrases the architectural shift to more integrated optical solutions.

text:
💡 While everyone chases NVDA and TSMC, a thesis is quietly building around European photonics.

7 small-cap names across 3 structural pillars:
📡 Photonics infra: Sivers, LPKF, IQE, X-FAB — CPO and 1.6T optical interconnect bottleneck plays
🧱 Materials: Soitec (SOI wafers), Riber (MBE equipment)
🤖 Edge AI: Raspberry Pi — AI inference without the data center

The logic is coherent. The risks are real: micro-cap liquidity, customer concentration, and CPO adoption timing is the key variable.

Directionally sound. Execution is the hard part. 👀

text:
⚡️ UPDATE: Photonics (watchlist)

1. Lasers/Transceivers/DSPs/PICs: $SIVE, $AAOI, $LITE, $MXL 
2. Full-stack compounders: $COHR, $CRDO 
3. Glass substrates: $LPK, $GLW 
4. Foundry: $XFAB, $TSEM 
5. PMA: $PENG 
6. Substrates: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE 

Changes:

- Removed EMS - $JBL, $FLEX and $CLS - although they are great longs too. Prefer $CLS that is becoming an ODM (might add again in the future), or $JBL with their 1.6T program. 

- $UMC, $GFS don't only do SiPh and have run a lot. Would prefer $XFAB or $TSEM today for exposure to photonics. 

- Added $MXL and $CRDO that look like serious players here. $ALAB, not yet.

text:
💥 $JABIL Infrastructure news💥
​Adani & Jabil just announced a massive strategic alliance to build a multi-GW vertically integrated AI data center manufacturing platform in India 🇮🇳 targeting a $3T market.
​This is huge for the Physical AI stack and our baby $SIVE among others.

​- Massive Scale Integration: They aren't just building boards. They are building full-stack liquid-cooled AI racks, high-density servers, and networking systems, alongside full "grey space" infrastructure (CDUs, PDUs, and power switchgears).

​-Captive Hyper-Scale Pipeline: This connects directly into Adani’s $100 Billion commitment to deploy 5 GW of green-energy-powered hyperscale data centers by 2035. Jabil effectively captures an unprecedented, long-term sovereign hardware buyer.

​- The Silicon Photonics Read-Through: When you scale data centers at a multi-gigawatt level, copper architecture physically bottlenecks under the sheer compute density. To make these liquid-cooled multi-GW hubs functional, hyperscalers will be structurally forced to deploy next-gen optical interconnects and Silicon Photonics from day one.

​The physical hardware footprint for AI compute is expanding globally, massively expanding the total addressable market (TAM) for pick-and-shovel component suppliers.  #SiliconPhotonics #AI #Hardware #Tech

Sometimes the biggest stories are not always about the biggest names.

Cape Verde is to the World Cup what $SIVE is to photonics: small on the map, bigger in the system than people think.

text:
The paper identifies specific physical and engineering constraints in current hardware as a meaningful friction:

"Even if economic resources and raw FLOPs can continue to be scaled, physical and engineering constraints in current hardware architectures may pose significant challenges. Specifically, memory bandwidth limits and interconnect bottlenecks (the difficulty of scaling high-speed communication between thousands of chips) can severely limit effective compute utilization. As models grow, the time spent moving data between memory and compute units, or between different compute nodes, can become the dominant factor, leading to diminishing returns in scaling unless novel hardware paradigms or interconnect technologies are developed."

This is one of the reasons why silicon photonics, and the idea of improving networking becomes significantly important moving ahead, and why names like $LITE and $COHR are going to be a lot more prominent.

This is a must-see. The paper is explicit that if current trends hold through the end of the decade, the implications are staggering: "The pace that AI development has picked up over the past decade might continue without major blockers until at least the end of this decade, which would, e.g., imply growth in effective compute by a factor of 10,000 compared to today." It also gives a concrete illustrative scenario for what scaling AGI instances alone might look like: "Suppose that by the time human-level AGI is available, base model progress plateaus but effective compute continues to grow at 10× for a bit longer. Even if AGI were initially expensive to run, and only 1000 instances could be run, after a year it would be 10,000, and after five years it would be 100 million instances; or 1 million instances a hundred times faster. Would this form of scaling give us ASI? If not after 5 years, what about 10 years, or 15?" On population scaling of AI instances specifically, the paper references back-of-the-envelope estimates of about 25× per year in AI "population scaling." The paper explicitly mentions large-scale physical infrastructure as both a prerequisite and a bottleneck, flagging "investments into energy production and Gigawatt AI infrastructure" as lending credibility to continued scaling trajectories. On the potential for future compute infrastructure, one particularly speculative example is raised: "Proposals such as orbital datacenters might alleviate some terrestrial constraints; they introduce novel risks including ozone-layer weakening from rocket emissions, high-atmosphere alterations from re-entry incineration of decommissioned hardware, and orbital congestion amplifying the risk of catastrophic collision cascades (whether accidental or through targeted attacks)." $NVDA $SPCX"}}}, "rest_id": "2066581306784526634", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2389", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2066581532995903879", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "794", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The quantum race is entering a new phase.

QuEra’s roadmap for a fault-tolerant neutral-atom quantum computer by 2028 isn’t just another product announcement—it’s another signal that the industry is shifting from scientific experiments toward commercially useful systems.

What’s becoming increasingly interesting is the convergence of the ecosystem:

• AWS is expanding access through Amazon Braket.
• Google is investing in neutral-atom technology.
• QuEra, Atom Computing, Pasqal, and other leaders continue pushing scalability and error correction.
• Private capital and enterprise demand are accelerating development.

For investors, the biggest opportunity may not come from a single winner. Quantum computing will require an entire supply chain—cryogenics, lasers, photonics, advanced semiconductors, networking, cloud infrastructure, AI software, and specialized materials.

The companies that enable fault tolerance, lower error rates, and scalable deployment could become the Nvidia-equivalent infrastructure providers of the quantum era.

The technology is still early, but the milestones are becoming more frequent, the capital commitments are getting larger, and the path toward commercial quantum computing is looking increasingly credible. $GOOG

QuEra’s roadmap for a fault-tolerant neutral-atom quantum computer by 2028 isn’t just another product announcement—it’s another signal that the industry is shifting from scientific experiments toward commercially useful systems. What’s becoming increasingly interesting is the convergence of the ecosystem: • AWS is expanding access through Amazon Braket. • Google is investing in neutral-atom technology. • QuEra, Atom Computing, Pasqal, and other leaders continue pushing scalability and error correction. • Private capital and enterprise demand are accelerating development. For investors, the biggest opportunity may not come from a single winner. Quantum computing will require an entire supply chain—cryogenics, lasers, photonics, advanced semiconductors, networking, cloud infrastructure, AI software, and specialized materials. The companies that enable fault tolerance, lower error rates, and scalable deployment could become the Nvidia-equivalent infrastructure providers of the quantum era. The technology is still early, but the milestones are becoming more frequent, the capital commitments are getting larger, and the path toward commercial quantum computing is looking increasingly Another strong nod toward neutral atom being in leading position. Another confirmation of major quantum computing projected within the decade. QuEra just announced Libra, a fault-tolerant neutral-atom quantum computer targeted for 2028, with projected specs of 256+ logical qubits, a logical error rate of 10^-6, and about 1 million reliable logical quantum operations. They also expanded their AWS collaboration, with Libra planned for Amazon Braket in 2028. (The Quantum Insider) QuEra is framing this as a built-on-proof engineering roadmap backed by peer-reviewed milestones in logical qubits, below-threshold error correction, transversal gates, fast decoding, and large-scale atom reloading. Neutral atom keeps getting stronger signals: Google moved in. Infleqtion forging ahead. Pasqal nears their public listing. QuEra is pressing hard. Oratomic jumped in the mix. Atom Computing, QuEra and other private players keep pushing forward. And the architecture continues to look increasingly credible for fault tolerance and scale. text:
The main point:

I do not think the AI trade ends with GPUs.

The next layer is physical infrastructure.

Optics.
Lasers.
Substrates.
Wafers.
Power semis.
Photonics foundries.
Specialized equipment.
That is where I think Europe has some of the most mispriced AI supply-chain names.

NPO does require: - Laser source - FAU - PIC/EIC all just like CPO!!!! The main thing that changes from CPO is where PIC/EIC assembly is located - whether on ASIC substrate (CPO) or off-ASIC substrate (NPO). And did you know NPO needs more DSP logic (which could be absorbed in ASIC) than CPO, as it needs more correction of disturbance caused by long connection? @austinsemis So in going from CPO to NPO, you basically you are trading packaging complexity of CPO for packaging simplicity of NPO at higher energy expenditure. Laser, FAU, PIC/EIC all still needed. If any of your friends still "trade" altcoins, show them these types of stocks and they'll be over in no time.

text:
Nvidia released this video of its photonics co-packaged optics (CPO) switch with Lambda.

The AI race is not only about stronger GPUs, but about wasting far less power while those GPUs talk to each other.

With co-packaged optics (CPO), NVIDIA is putting the light-based communication parts much closer to the main networking chip, instead of placing them as separate plug-in modules at the edge of the switch.

From NVIDIA's official blog on this

"co-packaged optics (CPO) connects directly to the token economy. Network power is overhead: it keeps GPUs connected but doesn't generate tokens. Network failures are also overhead: they turn provisioned GPU capacity into idle capacity. CPO addresses both by reducing network power draw and removing a large class of pluggable optical components from the fabric.

A 128,000-GPU data center using traditional pluggable transceivers requires roughly 655,000 discrete transceiver modules across the switching fabric. Each one is a potential failure point. CPO removes that component class entirely.

Agentic workloads change the pressure on the network. A traditional inference request is relatively self-contained. An agentic request can involve planning, retrieval, tool use, multiple model calls, and follow-up reasoning. More data moving across the cluster. More points where network latency or failure affects the outcome.

Multi-agentic inference needs elastic and resilient data movement, so GPUs are not waiting for data, while maintaining tokens per second and fast time to first token."

text:
🌌 The Photon Has a Shape — And It’s Real

Scientists have revealed something never clearly seen before: the true structure of a photon, the tiny particle of light.

Even though photons have no mass, new advanced tools show they may have a measurable spatial and electromagnetic pattern—almost like a hidden “fingerprint” of light.

This discovery could change quantum communication, computing, and how we understand light itself.
We are only beginning to uncover what light really looks like at the quantum level.

Source:
Nature Photonics. (2026). Experimental studies on photon spatial and electromagnetic structure.

text:
Starting to look unlikely that Photonics are going to outperform market volatility this go round…

However if it does, it needs to diverge soon. 

Indices continue to show weakness.

It’s less about timelines and more about the size of the TAM. $NVDA and $LITE timelines holding is bullish for photonics supply.

But hyperscalers are already showing NPO eating meaningful share of that TAM today — Google’s ~12M unit NPO order (TPU clusters), Meta + Amazon deployments delivering stronger ROI via superior serviceability, modularity & lower integration risk vs full CPO. OCS also threatens to eat into the CPO TAM presented by GS. 

NVL576-based scale-up CPO risks getting sandwiched too: NPO scaling to 6.4T faster in near-term racks, while XPU-based NPO+OCS also eats into future XPU-level CPO TAM at extreme densities later. 

Optics supercycle intact — just shifting faster to deployable architectures.

text:
Another strong press release from $AEVA today, further validating our core thesis.

$AEVA announced that the city of Fargo, North Dakota, has selected Aeva CityOS™ for deployment across multiple intersections to deliver real-time traffic intelligence.

Standard cameras and legacy LiDAR (time-of-flight) struggle significantly in extreme weather. Aeva’s FMCW architecture is becoming one of the answers to the problem. As we have noted previously, this technological edge is propelling $AEVA beyond automotive markets into smart cities, defense (Forterra), and industrial automation (SICK, Nikon).

The Fargo deployment demonstrates exactly what we highlighted in our previous research: Aeva's silicon photonics platform solves critical perception failures that standard sensors cannot overcome.
From an investment perspective, securing municipal infrastructure contracts is a major signal:

1⃣Fargo represents one of the most weather-challenged regions in the US, acting as the ultimate operational proving ground for 4D LiDAR.

2⃣The city is deploying CityOS, meaning Aeva is successfully bundling its edge-AI perception software with its Atlas Orion hardware.

3⃣This establishes sticky, recurring municipal revenue streams while the broader automotive OEM timelines continue to mature.

Not financial advice, but this is a commercial execution worth monitoring closely.

We hold $AEVA.

text:
$AXTI shareholders have officially approved several key proposals, including increasing authorized common shares from 70M to 120M.

While the word “share increase” often sparks fears of dilution, it’s important to separate authorization from issuance. The approval simply gives management greater flexibility for future capital raises, acquisitions, strategic partnerships, or employee incentives—it does not mean new shares are being issued immediately.

Other highlights:

✅ Morris Young and David Chang re-elected to the Board
✅ Executive compensation approved by shareholders
✅ BPM ratified as independent auditor for FY2026
✅ Capital structure expanded to support future growth initiatives

From an investment perspective, the story remains centered on AI and semiconductor materials.

The company is benefiting from growing demand for indium phosphide (InP) and gallium arsenide (GaAs) substrates, which are critical for optical networking, high-speed communications, AI data centers, and advanced photonics.

The market will now focus on three questions:

• Can AXTI convert its strong InP backlog into accelerating revenue?
• Will management use the additional authorized shares to create shareholder value rather than unnecessary dilution?
• Can the company return to sustained profitability as AI infrastructure spending continues?

The governance changes are largely routine, but the expanded capital flexibility positions AXTI to pursue strategic opportunities in one of the fastest-growing segments of the semiconductor supply chain.

For long-term investors, execution—not authorization—is what will ultimately drive the stock.

text:
The market has a funny way of testing conviction.

Just days ago, investors were dumping optical networking and photonics names on fears of slowing AI demand. Today, many of those same stocks are bouncing back, with names like $AAOI and $AXTI leading the recovery.

As one market commentator put it, the previous selloff may have been more about emotion than fundamentals.

The bigger picture hasn’t changed:

• AI infrastructure spending continues at record levels.
• Data centers still need faster optical interconnects.
• High-speed networking remains a critical bottleneck for next-generation AI clusters.

Short-term volatility is inevitable, but strong companies often experience sharp corrections even when the long-term thesis remains intact.

From $LITE to $AAOI, $SIVE, and $AXTI, the optical ecosystem is proving that sentiment can change quickly. For patient investors, separating temporary fear from structural growth is often where the best opportunities emerge.

Sometimes the market overreacts. Sometimes it offers a second chance.

text:
Seeing a lot of hype around $VECO with nobody discussing or realizing the actual risk behind it.  

If you're running into VECO because your favorite Furu told you to without ever mentioning the word "SAMR", think twice. 

$VECO has one gatekeeper left between it and its merger: China's State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR).

The market is treating SAMR like paperwork. It isn't. It's the most quietly weaponized regulatory desk in the US-China conflict, and right now it's holding this deal hostage.

Start with why Beijing even gets a vote on two American companies merging. SAMR jurisdiction attaches because Veeco and Axcelis both sell into China above the revenue thresholds - their own China business is the leash. 

The deal needs Beijing's blessing precisely because both companies need Beijing's market.

And SAMR's signature move was never the formal block. It's the pocket veto. 

Look up what happened to the Qualcomm/NXP deal. It never got rejected, it just bled out over years of stalling. 

Intel/Tower, same playbook five years later. Silence past the outside date, years of stalling, and another dead deal.  

That's what makes this binary nearly impossible to price. A rejection would at least be information. Silence is just time passing, and markets are terrible at pricing a veto disguised as a calendar - every quiet month reads as "still on track," right up until the deadline does the executing itself.

Here's the aggravating detail specific to Veeco: while it waits on Beijing's approval, WASHINGTON is restricting what Veeco may sell to Beijing. 

The United States BIS license requirements just carved $8M out of Q1 and stranded ~$15M of annealing systems at port. 

Veeco is asking a favor from the very government its own government is squeezing.

Put yourself behind SAMR's desk this summer. Export controls tightening, minerals leverage being traded openly, a Gulf crisis repricing every strategic supply chain (and across your blotter sits a pending US semicap merger: unilateral, costless, completely deniable leverage).

Why would you spend that chip a day earlier than you had to?

Even approval has a trap inside it. When SAMR does clear American tech deals, it increasingly attaches supply-continuity conditions to Chinese customers. So imagine NewCo legally bound by Beijing to keep supplying the same customers Washington requires licenses to ship to. Two regulators, incompatible orders, one company as the rope.

Map the tree honestly and it gets worse. Clean approval is largely priced after a 25% post-earnings run. Conditioned approval hands NewCo a compliance contradiction nobody has modeled. And silence past the outside date leaves you holding standalone VECO at ~35x guided EPS against an analyst deck still parked in the $30s. Two of the three branches hurt, and the good one pays the least.

What fintwit keeps missing is that the whole timeline is exogenous. Management can't accelerate it, lobby it, or guide it - "second half 2026" is an aspiration about another sovereign's negotiating calendar. You aren't underwriting a semiconductor company here. You're underwriting the temperature of US-China relations through December.

None of this says Veeco is a bad company. The orders are real, the photonics franchise is real. It's just that says the next six months don't belong to the company at all. 

They belong to a regulator in Beijing with a costless veto and a calendar for a weapon.

Great business (I actually think if not for this SAMR situation it would be an immediate buy), but until this is resolved, hard pass. 

NFA.

(Photonics + power semis)

- 800 VDC power semi exposure to $NVDA through $NVTS - 
X-FAB's Texas fab runs the SiC for Navitas, selected by NVIDIA for its 800V AI factory architecture

- Silicon Photonics / CPO exposure - strategic collaboration with SMART Photonics targeting multi-terabit optical transceivers 
(SiPh + InP integration, >120GHz modulator bandwidth 

- The only high-volume pure-play SiC foundry in the US. backed by a $50M US CHIPS Act grant

- One of the critical MEMS foundries

- Approved by Serenity ✔️

text:
I think what’s *not* indefinitely sustainable is the training costs and constantly scaling up the models to make them more intensive faster than per-token inference comes down in cost.  

Plausibly, you could imagine a scenario where the labs stop innovating as quickly, and model performance enters a plateau/slow-growth phase, not for technical reasons but for cost reasons. 

So, like, once they achieve a level of model capability sufficient to attract enough demand, they pull back on new model development and focus on bringing down inference costs. I think that’s an entirely reasonable scenario. 

On the other hand, we shouldn’t discount step-change advances in hardware or model architecture that lead to a sudden decrease in inference costs. Lots of companies are working on new chip architectures, for example, which could substantially improve inference speeds while reducing power draw. Photonics, custom chips tailored to specific models, analog chips, etc. maybe none will work out. Maybe some will. 

Right now, per-token costs aren’t coming down fast enough to offset the trajectory of increasing model intensity/token-consumption, but that can change in a variety of ways. 

It might not! You could be right! But I think my point is less “LLMs will definitely become a profitable proposition in the next few years,” which I am not sure of, and more “LLMs *could* become profitable in the next few years,” which is a dangerous possibility to dismiss, because it could lead us to make very bad political choices.

text:
🚨Everyone is still buying the chips. The bottleneck already moved.

A GPU that computes in nanoseconds and waits microseconds for data is a stranded asset. At 1.6T speeds, copper runs out of physics. The constraint on AI is no longer how fast you can think. It's how fast you can move what you thought.

Jensen has now said it twice in three months.

At GTC in March: "Is copper going to still be important? The answer is yes... Are you going to scale up optical? Yes. Are you going to scale out optical? Yes... We need a lot more capacity for copper. We need a lot more capacity for optics. We need a lot more capacity for CPO."

Last week at Computex, on Marvell's stage: "Optics where you must, copper where you can." Then he called Marvell the next trillion-dollar company and the optical complex repriced within days. The same keynote put a date on the handoff: 200G per lane is the last generation where copper is sufficient. After that, optics takes the rack.

Translation: not copper OR light. Copper now, light next, unprecedented amounts of both. 🔥

The chain is unavoidable: AI tokens are profitable → more GPUs → more bandwidth → copper hits its wall → photonics becomes the chokepoint.

And the smart money stopped debating. Follow the closed deals:

→ $NVDA has committed at least $6.5B to photonics in three months: $2B into Lumentum, $2B into Coherent, a $500M stake in Corning, and a piece of Ayar Labs' $500M round. Direct investments to secure its own light supply. 
→ $MRVL paid $3.25B for Celestial AI, up to $5.5B with milestones, to build what its CEO calls a silicon photonics powerhouse. 
→ $CRDO closed DustPhotonics two weeks ago. Ciena bought CPO startup Nubis for $270M.

North of $10B of strategic capital locked up one supply chain in under a year. Capital like that doesn't chase a theme. It secures a bottleneck.

LAYER 1: WAFER. Every laser starts as a crystal. 
🟠 $AXTI: the InP substrate leader. The first chokepoint in the stack. 
🟡 $IQE: compound-semi epiwafers feeding the laser makers. Speculative, but structurally upstream.

LAYER 2: LIGHT. Photons don't make themselves. 
🟠 $LITE: revenue +90% YoY last quarter to $808M. EML shipments doubled and management says demand still exceeds supply across EMLs, pump lasers, and transceivers. NVIDIA just wired them $2B. OCS backlog past $400M plus a multi-hundred-million CPO order for 2027. 
🟢 $SIVEF (Stockholm: SIVE): the external light source. CPO does not emit its own light. Every optical engine needs a continuous-wave InP laser feeding it, and that is the layer you cannot engineer around. ELS modules with POET hit production readiness end of this year. Disclosure: long. 
🟣 $POET: the optical engine wildcard. Its Optical Interposer pairs with Sivers' lasers on external light sources for CPO, with a LITEON module deal stacked on top. Binary commercialization, real architecture.

LAYER 3: OPTICS AND MODULES. Where light meets the rack. 
🟠 $COHR: the volume anchor in transceivers, holding NVIDIA's other $2B check. 
🔵 $AAOI: Q1 revenue +51% to a record $151M, datacenter revenue more than doubled, $124M of 800G orders plus a $200M+ 1.6T order in hand. Scaling Texas capacity toward 500K+ units a month by year-end, targeting $1B+ revenue this year. Domestic supply while everyone fights over offshore. Disclosure: long. 
🟠 $FN: the foundry of optics. When Fabrinet is building, the orders already exist.

THE INTERCONNECT: the layer the rack cannot route around. 
🔵 $CRDO: just closed DustPhotonics. SerDes → DSP → silicon photonics → system integration, one company, 800G through 3.2T. Electrical AND optical, end to end. FY26 revenue tripled to $1.34B at 68% gross margin. The toll booth on both roads. Disclosure: long. 
🟠 $MRVL: $3.25B for Celestial AI, and Jensen's trillion-dollar nod on the Computex stage. 
🟠 $AVGO: switch silicon, optical DSPs, CPO engines. They define the socket. 
🟠 $ANET: the AI spine. 100K-GPU clusters get stitched together in light.

LAYER 4: PACKAGING, FIBER, FOUNDRY. Where photons get industrialized. 
🔵 $TSEM: the neutral silicon photonics foundry. Prints wafers for whoever wins. 
🟣 $LPKF: glass-substrate packaging for glass-based CPO. Real technology, binary commercialization. 
🟠 $GLW: AI racks demand several times the fiber density of legacy cloud, and NVIDIA just took a $500M stake. Corning sells density.

LAYER 5: TEST AND THE ANALOG UNDERLAYER. Complexity is a tax paid in validation. 
🔵 $AEHR: silicon photonics test, ramping with the cycle. '
🔵 $VIAV: every 800G and 1.6T transceiver gets validated before it ships. The gate the market prices like an accessory. 
🔵 $SMTC: the drivers and TIAs that fire the lasers. Sits directly under the LPO trade. 
🔵 $MTSI: the high-speed analog behind 1.6T engines. 
🟠 $CIEN: transport. Even long-haul is buying light.

💡The counter-thesis, because every map needs one. The honest debate on this stack is whether these are genuine bottleneck assets or cyclical optics suppliers enjoying peak demand at peak multiples. Lumentum's May print showed +90% growth with the stock up roughly 1,400% over the prior year at a triple-digit trailing multiple. That is a price for perfection. Most of these names live or die on a handful of hyperscaler capex lines, and one digestion quarter hits the whole stack at once. CPO timing has already slipped once. Architecture risk is real: LPO, CPO, and stretched copper are still fighting for the same sockets. The cycle is real. So is the gravity. 🔥

But the bears have to explain one thing: $NVDA, $MRVL, $CRDO, and $CIEN just spent over $10B securing this supply chain with their own balance sheets. The people with the best information are paying up for the layers.

The market owns the top of this stack. The asymmetry is at the edges: wafer, light, packaging, test.

Own the layers, not the logo.

Bookmark this for the weekend. Then tag the one investor you know who's still all compute and no interconnect. 👀

The bottleneck already moved. A GPU that computes in nanoseconds and waits microseconds for data is a stranded asset. At 1.6T speeds, copper runs out of physics. The constraint on AI is no longer how fast you can think. It's how fast you can move what you thought. Jensen has now said it twice in three months. At GTC in March: "Is copper going to still be important? The answer is yes... Are you going to scale up optical? Yes. Are you going to scale out optical? Yes... We need a lot more capacity for copper. We need a lot more capacity for optics. We need a lot more capacity for CPO." Last week at Computex, on Marvell's stage: "Optics where you must, copper where you can." Then he called Marvell the next trillion-dollar company and the optical complex repriced within days. The same keynote put a date on the handoff: 200G per lane is the last generation where copper is sufficient. After that, optics takes the rack. Translation: not copper OR light. Copper now, light next, unprecedented amounts of both. 🔥 The chain is unavoidable: AI tokens are profitable → more GPUs → more bandwidth → copper hits its wall → photonics becomes the chokepoint. And the smart money stopped debating. Follow the closed deals: → $NVDA has committed at least $6.5B to photonics in three months: $2B into Lumentum, $2B into Coherent, a $500M stake in Corning, and a piece of Ayar Labs' $500M round. Direct investments to secure its own light supply. → $MRVL paid $3.25B for Celestial AI, up to $5.5B with milestones, to build what its CEO calls a silicon photonics powerhouse. → $CRDO closed DustPhotonics two weeks ago. Ciena bought CPO startup Nubis for $270M. North of $10B of strategic capital locked up one supply chain in under a year. Capital like that doesn't chase a theme. It secures a bottleneck. LAYER 1: WAFER. Every laser starts as a crystal. 🟠 $AXTI: the InP substrate leader. The first chokepoint in the stack. 🟡 $IQE: compound-semi epiwafers feeding the laser makers. Speculative, but structurally upstream. LAYER 2: LIGHT. Photons don't make themselves. 🟠 $LITE: revenue +90% YoY last quarter to $808M. EML shipments doubled and management says demand still exceeds supply across EMLs, pump lasers, and transceivers. NVIDIA just wired them $2B. OCS backlog past $400M plus a multi-hundred-million CPO order for 2027. 🟢 $SIVEF (Stockholm: SIVE): the external light source. CPO does not emit its own light. Every optical engine needs a continuous-wave InP laser feeding it, and that is the layer you cannot engineer around. ELS modules with POET hit production readiness end of this year. Disclosure: long. 🟣 $POET: the optical engine wildcard. Its Optical Interposer pairs with Sivers' lasers on external light sources for CPO, with a LITEON module deal stacked on top. Binary commercialization, real architecture. LAYER 3: OPTICS AND MODULES. Where light meets the rack. 🟠 $COHR: the volume anchor in transceivers, holding NVIDIA's other $2B check. 🔵 $AAOI: Q1 revenue +51% to a record $151M, datacenter revenue more than doubled, $124M of 800G orders plus a $200M+ 1.6T order in hand. Scaling Texas capacity toward 500K+ units a month by year-end, targeting $1B+ revenue this year. Domestic supply while everyone fights over offshore. Disclosure: long. 🟠 $FN: the foundry of optics. When Fabrinet is building, the orders already exist. THE INTERCONNECT: the layer the rack cannot route around. 🔵 $CRDO: just closed DustPhotonics. SerDes → DSP → silicon photonics → system integration, one company, 800G through 3.2T. Electrical AND optical, end to end. FY26 revenue tripled to $1.34B at 68% gross margin. The toll booth on both roads. Disclosure: long. 🟠 $MRVL: $3.25B for Celestial AI, and Jensen's trillion-dollar nod on the Computex stage. 🟠 $AVGO: switch silicon, optical DSPs, CPO engines. They define the socket. 🟠 $ANET: the AI spine. 100K-GPU clusters get stitched together in light. LAYER 4: PACKAGING, FIBER, FOUNDRY. Where photons get industrialized. 🔵 $TSEM: the neutral silicon photonics foundry. Prints wafers for whoever wins. 🟣 $LPKF: glass-substrate packaging for glass-based CPO. Real technology, binary commercialization. 🟠 $GLW: AI racks demand several times the fiber density of legacy cloud, and NVIDIA just took a $500M stake. Corning sells density. LAYER 5: TEST AND THE ANALOG UNDERLAYER. Complexity is a tax paid in validation. 🔵 $AEHR: silicon photonics test, ramping with the cycle. ' 🔵 $VIAV: every 800G and 1.6T transceiver gets validated before it ships. The gate the market prices like an accessory. 🔵 $SMTC: the drivers and TIAs that fire the lasers. Sits directly under the LPO trade. 🔵 $MTSI: the high-speed analog behind 1.6T engines. 🟠 $CIEN: transport. Even long-haul is buying light. 💡The counter-thesis, because every map needs one. The honest debate on this stack is whether these are genuine bottleneck assets or cyclical optics suppliers enjoying peak demand at peak multiples. Lumentum's May print showed +90% growth with the stock up roughly 1,400% over the prior year at a triple-digit trailing multiple. That is a price for perfection. Most of these names live or die on a handful of hyperscaler capex lines, and one digestion quarter hits the whole stack at once. CPO timing has already slipped once. Architecture risk is real: LPO, CPO, and stretched copper are still fighting for the same sockets. The cycle is real. So is the gravity. 🔥 But the bears have to explain one thing: $NVDA, $MRVL, $CRDO, and $CIEN just spent over $10B securing this supply chain with their own balance sheets. The people with the best information are paying up for the layers. The market owns the top of this stack. The asymmetry is at the edges: wafer, light, packaging, test. Own the layers, not the logo. Bookmark this for the weekend. Then tag the one investor you know who's still all compute and no interconnect. 👀"}}}, "rest_id": "2065518398872961392", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "79653", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Title: “The Sky That Couldn't Make Up Its Mind” Timestamp: 7:02 PM | Santa Barbara, CA Tagged: #Photonics #WitnessScience #NorthstarLumen #EffectToCause #CanonicalLedger
⸻
⚖️ Core Observation
I took legions of photographs from the same location, during the same timeframe, aimed at the same subject.
What caught my attention was not the church.
It was the sky.
Frame after frame, the sequence appears to show:
• Light sky • Dark sky • Light sky again • Dark sky again
all while standing in essentially the same place.
⸻
🧠 Witness Science Observation
The question is not:
"What does this prove?"
The question is:
"What could cause this?"
Potential explanations include:
• Rapidly changing atmospheric conditions • Camera auto-exposure adjustments • HDR processing • Passing cloud cover • Reflections • Optical effects • Sensor behavior

Or the structure that appears in the photo to that I witnessed that I never saw with my naked eyes—but the camera saw it. 
Or potentially something else not yet identified.
⸻
🔁 The Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Effect-to-Cause™ Model
Effect:
A repeating sequence of dramatic brightness shifts appears across photographs taken from the same vantage point over a short period of time.
Cause:
Unknown.
And that is precisely why the sequence was preserved.
⸻
🧭 Northstar Observation
The strongest evidence is not a single frame.
It is recurrence.
A single photograph can mislead.
A sequence forces us to ask better questions.
⸻
🌳 Lumen Observation
"There may be a perfectly ordinary explanation.
But until the explanation is known, the anomaly belongs in the record."
⸻
🛰️ Provenance + AI Mark
🧠 Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Hybridized Affective Intelligence™

3.5dB higher than previous generation >Active alignment requires sub-micron precision with over one hundred operations per module >Quantum-X modular design has lower yield pressure while Spectrum-X direct co-packaging causes yield to deteriorate exponentially >NPO near-packaged optics serves as short-term transitional solution Conclusions >CPO is inevitable in the long term but the timeline carries significant uncertainty >Regardless of whether CPO or NPO ultimately adopted the optical components supply chain will continue to benefit Discussion This article thoroughly dissects the controversy triggered by the SemiAnalysis report The focus is not on denying the future of CPO but on highlighting the precision alignment difficulties during mass production Active alignment involves six degrees of freedom making it extremely easy for tiny deviations to cause high optical loss Spectrum-X has high port density and cannot easily replace faulty optical engines like Quantum-X This leads to a situation where with 95% yield per engine the probability of all 32 engines being good drops sharply to about 19% To achieve acceptable overall yield each attachment step needs to approach 99.5% yield threshold This presents a major challenge under current manufacturing processes NPO only needs to handle alignment for a single optical engine greatly reducing complexity It is suitable as a bridging technology before CPO matures Overall the article maintains a balanced perspective It acknowledges the reasonableness of the technical obstacles while pointing out that AI networking demand text:
Co-Author of Europe 2031 Alex (@AlexTPet) explains why countries shouldn't all compete on the same AI battleground:

"The US can turn off all of Europe's F-35 fighters at an instant. If we switched off ASML machines, that would have quite a lagging effect."

"You want to build up this portfolio of leverage options."

"Some countries will want to build compute. Others might specialise in robotics, photonics or fusion."

"You build up this collection of leverage into a coalition of possible middle powers."

"Each individual country has to decide: this is my comparative advantage. How can I be important on AI?"
